blocks:
Ayah

ٱلْحَمْدُ لِلَّهِ فَاطِرِ ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَٱلْأَرْضِ جَاعِلِ ٱلْمَلَـٰٓئِكَةِ رُسُلًا أُو۟لِىٓ أَجْنِحَةٍ مَّثْنَىٰ وَثُلَـٰثَ وَرُبَـٰعَ ۚ يَزِيدُ فِى ٱلْخَلْقِ مَا يَشَآءُ ۚ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ عَلَىٰ كُلِّ شَىْءٍ قَدِيرٌ (١)
All praise belongs to Allah, originator of the heavens and the earth, maker of the angels [His] messengers, possessing wings, two, three or four [of them]. He adds to the creation whatever He wishes. Indeed Allah has power over all things. (1)

(Commentary)

The purpose of the Surah is to expound the three principles: His Oneness, the Exalted, in His Lordship (الربوبية); the messengership of the Messenger; and the Return (المعاد) — the resurrection and final gathering of souls — to Him, as well as establishing the proof for these. To achieve this, it employs a series of His magnificent blessings, both celestial and terrestrial, and points toward His precise governance (تدبيره) of the affairs of the world in general and man in particular.

Prior to this detailed exposition, the Surah presents a summary indication that the opening and withholding of mercy (الرحمة) — which is the effusion of blessing (إفاضة النعمة) and its cessation — is restricted to Him, the Exalted, in His saying: ما يَفْتَحِ اللهُ لِلنَّاسِ مِنْ رَحْمَةٍ فَلا مُمْسِكَ لَها "Whatever mercy Allah unfolds for the people, no one can withhold it" (35:2).

Preceding even that is an indication of the intermediaries (وسائط) of this unfolded mercy and bestowed blessings, namely the angels (الملائكة) who mediate between Him, the Exalted, and His creation in carrying various types of blessings from Him and delivering them to His creation. Thus, He opened the Surah by mentioning them.

The Surah is Meccan (مكية), as indicated by the context (سياق) of its verses. Some have excepted two verses, namely His saying: إِنَّ الَّذِينَ يَتْلُونَ كِتابَ اللهِ "Indeed those who recite the Book of Allah..." (35:29) and His saying: ثُمَّ أَوْرَثْنَا الْكِتابَ الَّذِينَ اصْطَفَيْنا "Then We caused those whom We chose... to inherit the Book" (35:32), but this is not apparent from the context of the two verses.

His saying, the Exalted: الْحَمْدُ لِلَّهِ فاطِرِ السَّماواتِ وَالْأَرْضِ "All praise belongs to Allah, originator of the heavens and the earth" (35:1). Originating (الفطر) — according to what al-Raghib mentioned — is the longitudinal splitting. Applying the term "Originator" (الفاطر) to Him, the Exalted, is by way of a metaphorical consideration (عناية استعارية), as if He split non-existence (العدم) and brought forth from its belly the heavens and the earth. The sum of its meaning is that He is the bringer of the heavens and the earth into existence through an initial creation (إيجادا ابتدائيا) without a prior model. Thus, its meaning is close to the meaning of the "Inimitable Originator" (البديع) and the "Innovator" (المبدع). The difference between innovation (الإبداع) and origination (الفطر) is that the consideration in innovation relates to the negation of a prior model, while in origination, it relates to the repelling of non-existence and bringing the thing into existence from the start — unlike a craftsman who assembles different materials to manifest a new form that did not exist.

What is meant by "the heavens and the earth" is the totality of the witnessed world (العالم المشهود); thus it encompasses them and whatever creatures are within them. This would be of the category of mentioning the major parts while intending the whole metaphorically. Alternatively, it refers to the heavens and the earth themselves, out of regard for their status due to the greatness of their creation and the wonder of their affair, as He said: لَخَلْقُ السَّماواتِ وَالْأَرْضِ أَكْبَرُ مِنْ خَلْقِ النَّاسِ "Surely the creation of the heavens and the earth is greater than the creation of mankind" (40:57).

In any case, His saying: فاطِرِ السَّماواتِ وَالْأَرْضِ is among His names, the Exalted, used as an attribute for Allah. What is intended by the description is continuity (الاستمرار) rather than the past only, because bringing into existence is continuous and the effusion of existence (فيض الوجود) is uninterrupted; were it to be interrupted, all things would cease to exist.

The presentation of one description after another is to signal the reasons for the restriction of praise (الحمد) to Him, the Exalted. It is as if it were said: Praise belongs to Allah for His originating the heavens and the earth, and for His making the angels messengers possessing wings. Thus, He, the Exalted, is praised, for He has brought forth nothing but the beautiful.

His saying, the Exalted: جاعِلِ الْمَلائِكَةِ رُسُلاً أُولِي أَجْنِحَةٍ مَثْنى وَثُلاثَ وَرُباعَ "maker of the angels [His] messengers, possessing wings, two, three, and four [pairs]" (35:1). The angels (الملائكة) is the plural of malak (ملك). They are beings whom Allah created and made as intermediaries between Himself and the witnessed world. He has entrusted them with the ontological (التكوينية) and legislative (التشريعية) affairs of the world; they are honored servants who do not disobey Allah in what He commands them and they do what they are commanded.

So His saying, the Exalted: جاعِلِ الْمَلائِكَةِ رُسُلاً "maker of the angels [His] messengers" suggests, or rather indicates, that all angels — as "the angels" (الملائكة) is a plural defined by the definite article implying generality — are messengers and intermediaries between Him and His creation in the execution of His ontological and legislative commands.

There is no reason to restrict "messengers" in the verse to the angels who descend upon the prophets (peace be upon them). The Quran has applied the term "messengers" to other angels, such as His saying, the Exalted: حَتَّى إِذا جاءَ أَحَدَكُمُ الْمَوْتُ تَوَفَّتْهُ رُسُلُنا "until when death comes to one of you, Our messengers take him away" (6:61), and His saying: إِنَّ رُسُلَنا يَكْتُبُونَ ما تَمْكُرُونَ "Indeed Our messengers write down what you plot" (10:21), and His saying: وَلَمَّا جاءَتْ رُسُلُنا إِبْراهِيمَ بِالْبُشْرى قالُوا إِنَّا مُهْلِكُوا أَهْلِ هذِهِ الْقَرْيَةِ "And when Our messengers came to Abraham with the good news, they said, ‘We are indeed going to destroy the people of this town’" (29:31).

Wings (الأجنحة) is the plural of janah (جناح). In a bird, it is like the hand in a human; it is used as a means to ascend into the air, descend from it, and move from place to place by flying.

Thus, the existence of the angel is equipped with that by which it performs something analogous to what a bird performs with its wing. It moves by it from the heaven to the earth by the command of Allah, and ascends by it from the earth to the heaven, and from any place to any place. The Quran has named it a "wing" (جناحا), and this necessitates nothing more than the realization of the intended purpose of a wing. As for it being of the same nature as the wings of most birds, possessing feathers and down, the mere application of the word does not necessitate this, just as it did not necessitate it in similar instances like the terms Throne (العرش), Chair (الكرسي), Tablet (اللوح), Pen (القلم), and others.

His saying: أُولِي أَجْنِحَةٍ مَثْنى وَثُلاثَ وَرُباعَ "possessing wings, two, three, and four [pairs]" is an attribute of the angels. Mathna (مثنى), thulatha (ثلاث), and ruba‘a (رباع) are words indicating repeated numbers, i.e., two by two, three by three, and four by four. It is as if it were said: He made the angels, some possessing two wings, some possessing three wings, and some possessing four wings.

His saying: يَزِيدُ فِي الْخَلْقِ ما يَشاءُ "He adds to creation whatever He wishes" (35:1) is not devoid of a suggestion, according to the context, that among them are those whose wings exceed four.

His saying: إِنَّ اللهَ عَلى كُلِّ شَيْءٍ قَدِيرٌ "Indeed Allah has power over all things" (35:1) is a justification (تعليل) for all that preceded it, or for the final sentence, though the former is more apparent.

(Narrative Discussion)

In al-Bihar, from al-Ikhtisas, with its chain of transmission from al-Mu‘alla ibn Muhammad, reaching back to Abu ‘Abdillah (peace be upon him), who said: إن الله عز وجل خلق الملائكة من نور — "Indeed Allah, Mighty and Majestic, created the angels from light..." (the narration).

In the Tafsir of al-Qummi, Imam al-Sadiq (peace be upon him) said: خلق الله الملائكة مختلفة وقد أتى رسول الله صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله جبرئيل ـ وله ستمائة جناح على ساقه الدر ـ مثل القطر على البقل ـ قد ملأ ما بين السماء والأرض ـ وقال إذا أمر الله عز وجل ـ ميكائيل بالهبوط إلى الدنيا ـ صارت رجله في السماء السابعة ـ والأخرى في الأرض السابعة ، وإن لله ملائكة أنصافهم من برد وأنصافهم من نار ـ يقولون : يا مؤلفا بين البرد والنار ـ ثبت قلوبنا على طاعتك ـ. — "Allah created the angels varied. Gabriel came to the Messenger of Allah (peace be upon him and his progeny) having six hundred wings, with pearls upon his shanks like dew upon herbs, having filled what is between the heaven and the earth. He said: When Allah, Mighty and Majestic, commands Michael to descend to the world, one of his feet is in the seventh heaven and the other in the seventh earth. And indeed, Allah has angels whose halves are of hail and whose halves are of fire; they say: 'O You who have harmonized between hail and fire, make our hearts firm upon Your obedience.'"

He said: إن لله ملكا بعد ما بين شحمة أذنه إلى عينه ـ مسيرة خمسمائة عام بخفقان الطير ـ. — "Indeed, Allah has an angel, the distance between whose earlobe and his eye is a journey of five hundred years by the flight of a bird."

He said: إن الملائكة لا يأكلون ولا يشربون ولا ينكحون ـ وإنما يعيشون بنسيم العرش ، وإن لله عز وجل ملائكة ركعا إلى يوم القيامة ـ وإن لله عز وجل ملائكة سجدا إلى يوم القيامة ـ. — "Indeed, the angels do not eat, nor drink, nor marry; they only live by the breeze of the Throne. And indeed, Allah, Mighty and Majestic, has angels who are in bowing (ركعا) until the Day of Resurrection, and indeed, Allah, Mighty and Majestic, has angels who are in prostration (سجدا) until the Day of Resurrection."

Then Abu ‘Abdillah (peace be upon him) said: The Messenger of Allah (peace be upon him and his progeny) said: ما من شيء مما خلق الله عز وجل أكثر من الملائكة ـ وإنه ليهبط في كل يوم أو في كل ليلة ـ سبعون ألف ملك ، فيأتون البيت الحرام فيطوفون به ـ ثم يأتون رسول الله صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله ـ ثم يأتون أمير المؤمنين عليه‌السلام فيسلمون ـ ثم يأتون الحسين عليه‌السلام فيقيمون عنده ـ فإذا كان عند السحر وضع لهم معراج إلى السماء ـ ثم لا يعودون أبدا ـ. — "There is nothing among what Allah, Mighty and Majestic, created more numerous than the angels. Indeed, every day or every night, seventy thousand angels descend; they come to the Sacred House (البيت الحرام) and circumambulate it, then they come to the Messenger of Allah (peace be upon him and his progeny), then they come to the Commander of the Faithful (peace be upon him) and offer salutations, then they come to al-Husayn (peace be upon him) and stay with him. When it is dawn, a ladder (معراج) to heaven is placed for them, and they never return."

Abu Ja‘far (peace be upon him) said: إن الله عز وجل خلق إسرافيل وجبرائيل وميكائيل ـ من تسبيحة واحدة ، وجعل لهم السمع والبصر وجودة العقل وسرعة الفهم ـ. — "Indeed, Allah, Mighty and Majestic, created Seraphiel, Gabriel, and Michael from a single glorification (تسبيحة), and He gave them hearing, sight, excellence of intellect, and speed of understanding."

The Commander of the Faithful (peace be upon him) said regarding the creation of the angels: وملائكة خلقتهم وأسكنتهم سماواتك ـ فليس فيهم فترة ، ولا عندهم غفلة ، ولا فيهم معصية ، هم أعلم خلقك بك وأخوف خلقك منك ، وأقرب خلقك منك ، وأعملهم بطاعتك ، لا يغشاهم نوم العيون ولا سهو العقول ، ولا فترة الأبدان ـ لم يسكنوا الأصلاب ، ولم تضمهم الأرحام ، ولم تخلقهم من ماء مهين أنشأتهم إنشاء ـ فأسكنتهم سماواتك وأكرمتهم بجوارك ، وائتمنتهم على وحيك ، وجنبتهم الآفات ، ووقيتهم البليات ، وطهرتهم من الذنوب ، ولو لا قوتك لم يقووا ، ولو لا تثبيتك لم يثبتوا ، ولو لا رحمتك لم يطيعوا ، ولو لا أنت لم يكونوا ـ. — "And angels whom You created and settled in Your heavens—there is no languor (فترة) in them, nor is there heedlessness (غفلة) with them, nor is there disobedience among them. They are the most knowledgeable of Your creation concerning You, the most fearful of Your creation of You, the nearest of Your creation to You, and the most diligent of them in Your obedience. Neither the sleep of eyes nor the forgetfulness of intellects nor the languor of bodies overcomes them. They did not inhabit loins, nor did wombs enclose them, nor did You create them from a base fluid. You originated them as a [special] creation, settled them in Your heavens, honored them with Your proximity, entrusted them with Your revelation, kept them away from afflictions, protected them from calamities, and purified them from sins. Were it not for Your power, they would not be powerful; were it not for Your making them firm, they would not be firm; were it not for Your mercy, they would not obey; and were it not for You, they would not be."

أما إنهم على مكانتهم منك وطاعتهم إياك ـ ومنزلتهم عندك وقلة غفلتهم عن أمرك ـ لو عاينوا ما خفي عنهم منك لاحتقروا أعمالهم ، ولآزروا على أنفسهم ، ولعلموا أنهم لم يعبدوك حق عبادتك ـ سبحانك خالقا ومعبودا ـ ما أحسن بلاءك عند خلقك. — "Verily, despite their station with You, their obedience to You, their rank before You, and their lack of heedlessness regarding Your command—had they witnessed what is hidden from them of You, they would have belittled their deeds, blamed themselves, and known that they have not worshipped You with the worship You truly deserve. Glory be to You as Creator and Worshipped; how excellent is Your trial (بلاءك) with Your creation."

In al-Bihar, from al-Durr al-Manthur, from Abu al-‘Ala’ ibn Sa‘d: The Messenger of Allah (peace be upon him and his progeny) said one day to those sitting with him: أطت السماء وحق لها أن تئط ـ ليس منها موضع قدم إلا عليه ملك راكع أو ساجد. — "The heaven groaned, and it has every right to groan; there is not a foot's space within it except that there is an angel bowing or prostrating upon it." Then he recited: وَإِنَّا لَنَحْنُ الصَّافُّونَ وَإِنَّا لَنَحْنُ الْمُسَبِّحُونَ "And indeed we are those who stand in ranks, and indeed we are those who celebrate [His] glory" (37:165-166).

From al-Khisal, with its chain of transmission from Muhammad ibn Talhah, reaching back to the Prophet (peace be upon him and his progeny), who said: الملائكة على ثلاثة أجزاء فجزء لهم جناحان ـ وجزء لهم ثلاثة أجنحة وجزء لهم أربعة أجنحة. — "The angels are of three parts: a part having two wings, a part having three wings, and a part having four wings."

I say: It was narrated in al-Kafi with its chain from ‘Abdullah ibn Talhah similarly. Perhaps what is intended is a description of the majority of angels, so that it does not contradict the context of the verse and other narrations.

From al-Tawhid, with its chain from Abu Hayyan al-Taymi from his father from the Commander of the Faithful (peace be upon him), who said: ليس أحد من الناس إلا ومعه ملائكة حفظة ـ يحفظونه من أن يتردى في بئر ـ أو يقع عليه حائط أو يصيبه سوء ـ فإذا حان أجله خلوا بينه وبين ما يصيبه ـ — "There is no person among mankind but that with him are guardian angels (ملائكة حفظة) who protect him from falling into a well, or a wall falling upon him, or evil afflicting him. But when his appointed time (أجله) arrives, they leave him to what will afflict him..." (the narration).

From al-Basa'ir, from al-Sayyari from ‘Abdullah ibn Abi ‘Abdillah al-Farisi and others, reaching back to Abu ‘Abdillah (peace be upon him), who said: إن الكروبيين قوم من شيعتنا من الخلق الأول ـ جعلهم الله خلف العرش ـ لو قسم نور واحد منهم على أهل الأرض لكفاهم. — "Indeed, the Cherubim (الكروبيون) are a group of our followers (شيعتنا) from the first creation; Allah placed them behind the Throne. If the light of one of them were divided among the people of the earth, it would suffice them." Then he said: "When Moses (peace be upon him) asked his Lord what he asked, He commanded one of the Cherubim, and he manifested himself to the mountain and turned it into dust."

From al-Sahifah al-Sajjadiyyah, among his supplications for the Bearers of the Throne and every archangel: اللهم وحملة عرشك الذين لا يفترون من تسبيحك ، ولا يسأمون من تقديسك ، ولا يستحسرون عن عبادتك ، ولا يؤثرون التقصير على الجد في أمرك ، ولا يغفلون عن الوله إليك ، وإسرافيل صاحب الصور الشاخص ـ الذي ينتظر منك الإذن وحلول الأمر ـ فينبه بالنفخة صرعى رهائن القبور ، وميكائيل ذو الجاه عندك والمكان الرفيع من طاعتك ـ وجبريل الأمين على وحيك ـ المطاع في سماواتك المكين لديك المقرب عندك ، والروح الذي هو على ملائكة الحجب ـ والروح الذي هو من أمرك ـ. — "O Allah, and the Bearers of Your Throne, who never flag in glorifying You, nor tire of sanctifying You, nor weary of Your worship, nor prefer negligence over diligence in Your command, nor are heedless of their yearning for You. And Seraphiel, the master of the Trumpet, staring intently, awaiting Your permission and the arrival of the command, so that he may awaken with the blast those fallen as hostages of the graves. And Michael, the possessor of status with You and the high station through Your obedience. And Gabriel, the trustworthy over Your revelation, the one obeyed in Your heavens, the established with You, the near to You. And the Spirit (الروح) who is over the angels of the veils, and the Spirit who is of Your command."

اللهم فصل عليهم وعلى الملائكة الذين من دونهم ـ من سكان سماواتك وأهل الأمانة على رسالاتك ، والذين لا يدخلهم سأمة من دءوب ـ ولا إعياء من لغوب ولا فتور ـ ولا تشغلهم عن تسبيحك الشهوات ـ ولا يقطعهم عن تعظيمك سهو الغفلات ، الخشع الأبصار فلا يرومون النظر إليك ، النواكس الأذقان الذين قد طالت رغبتهم فيما لديك ـ المستهترون بذكر آلائك ـ والمتواضعون دون عظمتك وجلال كبريائك ، والذين يقولون إذا نظروا إلى جهنم ـ تزفر على أهل معصيتك ـ سبحانك ما عبدناك حق عبادتك ـ. — "O Allah, bless them and the angels below them—the inhabitants of Your heavens and the people of trust over Your messages; those whom no weariness from toil enters, nor exhaustion from fatigue, nor languor. Desires do not distract them from Your glorification, nor does the forgetfulness of heedlessness cut them off from Your magnification. Their eyes are humbled, so they do not seek to look at You; their chins are lowered, they whose longing for what is with You has been long. They are consumed with the remembrance of Your bounties and are humble before Your greatness and the majesty of Your pride. Those who say, when they look at Hell as it groans at the people of Your disobedience: 'Glory be to You, we have not worshipped You with the worship You truly deserve.'"

فصل عليهم وعلى الروحانيين من ملائكتك ـ وأهل الزلفة عندك ـ وحمال الغيب إلى رسلك والمؤتمنين على وحيك ـ وقبائل الملائكة الذين اختصصتهم لنفسك ـ وأغنيتهم عن الطعام والشراب بتقديسك ـ وأسكنتهم بطون أطباق سماواتك ، والذين هم على أرجائها إذا نزل الأمر بتمام وعدك ـ. — "So bless them and the spiritual ones (الروحانيين) among Your angels, the people of proximity to You, the carriers of the Unseen to Your messengers, the trustees over Your revelation, and the tribes of angels whom You have specialized for Yourself, and whom You have made independent of food and drink through Your sanctification, and whom You have settled within the layers of Your heavens, and those who will be upon its borders when the command descends for the fulfillment of Your promise."

وخزان المطر وزواجر السحاب ـ والذي بصوت زجره يسمع زجل الرعود ، وإذا سبحت به حفيفة السحاب التمعت صواعق البروق ، ومشيعي الثلج والبرد ـ والهابطين مع قطر المطر إذا نزل ، والقوام على خزائن الرياح ، والموكلين بالجبال فلا تزول ، والذين عرفتهم مثاقيل المياه ـ وكيل ما يحويه لواعج الأمطار وعوالجها ـ ورسلك من الملائكة إلى أهل الأرض ـ بمكروه ما ينزل من البلاء ومحبوب الرخاء ـ. — "And the treasurers of rain and the drivers of clouds—he by whose driving voice the rumble of thunders is heard, and when the rustling clouds are glorified by him, the bolts of lightning flash. And the escorts of snow and hail, and those who descend with the drops of rain when it falls. And those standing over the treasuries of the winds, and those entrusted with the mountains so they do not move. And those whom You have made to know the weights of the waters and the measure of what the torrential and surging rains contain. And Your messengers from the angels to the people of the earth with the disliked of descending affliction and the beloved of ease."

والسفرة الكرام البررة والحفظة الكرام الكاتبين ، وملك الموت وأعوانه ، ومنكر ونكير ، ومبشر وبشير ، ورؤمان فتان القبور ، والطائفين بالبيت المعمور ، ومالك والخزنة ، ورضوان وسدنة الجنان ، والذين لا يعصون الله ما أمرهم ويفعلون ما يؤمرون ، والذين يقولون : سلام عليكم بما صبرتم فنعم عقبى الدار ، والزبانية الذين إذا قيل لهم : « خذوه فغلوه ثم الجحيم صلوه » ابتدروه سراعا ولم ينظروه ، ومن ألهمنا ذكره ولم نعلم مكانه منه وبأي أمر وكلته ، وسكان الهواء والأرض والماء ، ومن منهم على الخلق ـ. — "And the noble and pious scribes (السفرة الكرام البررة), and the noble recording guardians (الحفظة الكرام الكاتبين), and the Angel of Death and his helpers, and Munkar and Nakir, and Mubashshir and Bashir, and Ruman the tester of graves, and those circumambulating the Frequented House (البيت المعمور), and Malik and the keepers [of Hell], and Ridwan and the attendants of the Gardens. And those who do not disobey Allah in what He commands them and do what they are commanded. And those who say: 'Peace be upon you for what you patiently endured; how excellent is the ultimate abode.' And the guards of Hell (الزبانية) who, when it is said to them: 'Seize him and fetter him, then cast him into Hell,' they rush to him quickly and do not grant him respite. And those whose mention You inspired us with but whose station with You we do not know, nor with what matter You entrusted them. And the inhabitants of the air, the earth, and the water, and those of them who are over the creation."

فصل عليهم يوم تأتي كل نفس معها سائق وشهيد ـ وصل عليهم صلاة تزيدهم كرامة على كرامتهم ـ وطهارة على طهارتهم. — "So bless them on the day when every soul comes, with it a driver and a witness. And bless them with a blessing that increases them in honor upon their honor and purity upon their purity." (The supplication).

In al-Bihar, from al-Durr al-Manthur, from Ibn Shihab: The Messenger of Allah (peace be upon him and his progeny) asked Gabriel to show himself to him in his [original] form. Gabriel said: "You will not be able to endure that." He said: "I would love that." So the Messenger of Allah (peace be upon him and his progeny) went out to the prayer-place on a moonlit night, and Gabriel came to him in his form. The Messenger of Allah (peace be upon him and his progeny) fainted when he saw him. Then he regained consciousness while Gabriel was supporting him, placing one of his hands on his chest and the other between his shoulders. The Messenger of Allah (peace be upon him and his progeny) said: ما كنت أرى أن شيئا ممن يخلق هكذا — "I did not think that anything among what is created was like this." Gabriel said: "How would it be if you saw Seraphiel? Indeed, he has twelve wings—a wing in the East and a wing in the West. The Throne is upon his shoulder, and at times he diminishes himself before the greatness of Allah until he becomes like a was‘ (1), such that nothing carries His Throne except His greatness."

In al-Safi, from al-Tawhid, with its chain from the Commander of the Faithful (peace be upon him) in a tradition, he said: Regarding His saying at the end of the verses: ما زاغَ الْبَصَرُ وَما طَغى ـ لَقَدْ رَأى مِنْ آياتِ رَبِّهِ الْكُبْرى "The eye did not swerve, nor did it transgress. Certainly he saw some of the greatest signs of his Lord" (53:17-18), he saw Gabriel in his form twice—this time and another time. That is because the creation of Gabriel is magnificent; he is among the spiritual ones (الروحانيين) whose creation and description none perceives except Allah.

From al-Khisal, with its chain from Muhammad ibn Marwan from Abu ‘Abdillah (peace be upon him), who said: The Messenger of Allah (peace be upon him and his progeny) said: إن جبرئيل أتاني فقال : إنا معشر الملائكة لا ندخل بيتا فيه كلب ـ ولا تمثال جسد ولا إناء يبال فيه. — "Indeed, Gabriel came to me and said: 'We, the assembly of angels, do not enter a house in which there is a dog, nor a statue of a body, nor a vessel in which one urinates.'"

I say: There are other narrations regarding the description of the angels beyond counting, mentioned in the section on the Return (المعاد), the Ascension (المعراج) of the Prophet (peace be upon him and his progeny), and other miscellaneous sections. What we have cited is a sufficient sample of that.

In al-‘Uyun, in the chapter on what has come from al-Rida (peace be upon him) of collected reports, with his chain from him (peace be upon him), he said: The Messenger of Allah (peace be upon him and his progeny) said: حسنوا القرآن بأصواتكم ـ فإن الصوت الحسن يزيد القرآن حسنا — "Beautify the Quran with your voices, for indeed a beautiful voice increases the beauty of the Quran," and he recited: يَزِيدُ فِي الْخَلْقِ ما يَشاءُ "He adds to creation whatever He wishes" (35:1).

In al-Tawhid, with its chain from Zurarah from ‘Abdullah ibn Sulayman from Abu ‘Abdillah (peace be upon him), who said: I heard him saying: إن القضاء والقدر خلقان من خلق الله ـ يزيد في الخلق ما يشاء. — "Indeed, the Decree (القضاء) and the Destiny (القدر) are two creations among the creations of Allah; He adds to creation whatever He wishes."

In al-Majma‘, regarding His saying, the Exalted: يَزِيدُ فِي الْخَلْقِ ما يَشاءُ "He adds to creation whatever He wishes" (35:1): Abu Hurayrah narrated from the Prophet (peace be upon him and his progeny) that he said: هو الوجه الحسن والصوت الحسن والشعر الحسن. — "It is the beautiful face, the beautiful voice, and the beautiful hair."

I say: The last three narrations are of the category of conceptual application (الجري والانطباق).

(1) With a fathah or sukun on the sad: a yellow bird smaller than a sparrow.

(A Discourse on the Angels)

The mention of the angels is repeated in the Noble Quran, and none of them are mentioned by name except Gabriel (جبريل) and Michael (ميكال). Others are mentioned by description, such as the Angel of Death (ملك الموت), the Noble Scribes (الكرام الكاتبين), the Noble and Pious Scribes (السفرة الكرام البررة), the Watcher (الرقيب), the Ready Observer (العتيد), and others.

What Allah, the Glorified, has mentioned in His speech — and which the preceding traditions support — regarding their attributes and actions is:

First: They are honored beings who are intermediaries between Him, the Exalted, and the witnessed world. There is no event or occurrence, small or large, but that the angels have a role in it, and there is an angel entrusted with it or angels entrusted with it, according to the aspect or aspects within it. They have no role in this except to execute the divine command in its course or to establish it in its place, as the Exalted said: لا يَسْبِقُونَهُ بِالْقَوْلِ وَهُمْ بِأَمْرِهِ يَعْمَلُونَ "They do not outstrip Him in speech, and they act by His command" (21:27).

Second: They do not disobey Allah in what He commands them. They do not possess an independent selfhood with an independent will that desires something other than what Allah, the Glorified, desires. Thus, they do not act independently, nor do they change a matter Allah has charged them with through alteration, addition, or subtraction. The Exalted said: لا يَعْصُونَ اللهَ ما أَمَرَهُمْ وَيَفْعَلُونَ ما يُؤْمَرُونَ "they do not disobey Allah in what He commands them, and they do what they are commanded" (66:6).

Third: The angels, despite their great number, are at different ranks of highness and lowness; some are above others and some are below others. Among them is the commander who is obeyed (آمر مطاع) and the commanded who obeys his command. The commander among them commands by the command of Allah, carrying it to the commanded, and the commanded is commanded by the command of Allah, obeying Him. They have nothing of themselves at all. The Exalted said: وَما مِنَّا إِلَّا لَهُ مَقامٌ مَعْلُومٌ "There is none among us but has a known station" (37:164), and He said: مُطاعٍ ثَمَّ أَمِينٍ "obeyed there and trustworthy" (81:21), and He said: قالُوا ما ذا قالَ رَبُّكُمْ قالُوا الْحَقَّ "They will say, ‘What did your Lord say?’ They will say, ‘The truth’" (34:23).

Fourth: They are never overcome, because they only act by the command and will of Allah. وَما كانَ اللهُ لِيُعْجِزَهُ مِنْ شَيْءٍ فِي السَّماواتِ وَلا فِي الْأَرْضِ "Nothing in the heavens or on the earth can frustrate Allah" (35:44). And Allah has said: وَاللهُ غالِبٌ عَلى أَمْرِهِ "and Allah is master of His affair" (12:21), and He said: إِنَّ اللهَ بالِغُ أَمْرِهِ "Indeed Allah carries through His command" (65:3).

From here it becomes clear that the angels are beings whose existence is sanctified from physical matter (المادة الجسمانية), which is subject to cessation, corruption, and change, and whose nature is gradual perfection (الاستكمال التدريجي) directed toward its goal—sometimes encountering obstacles and afflictions, thus being deprived of the goal and perishing before reaching it.

From here it also becomes clear that what has been reported in the narrations regarding the forms of the angels, their shapes, and their physical appearances — as a portion of which was presented in the preceding traditionary discussion — is only an exposition of their manifestations (تمثلاتهم) and appearances to those who described them among the prophets and Imams (peace be upon them). It is not a matter of [actual] formation (التصور) or shaping (التشكل). There is a difference between manifestation (التمثل) and shaping (التشكل). The manifestation of an angel as a human is its appearance to the one who witnesses it in the form of a human; thus, in the context of witnessing and perception, it possesses the form and shape of a human, while in itself and outside the context of perception, it is an angel possessing an angelic form. This is contrary to shaping and forming; for if it were to take the shape of a human and be formed in his form, it would become a human in itself, with no difference between the context of perception and what is outside it—it would be a human in reality (العين) and in the mind (الذهن) together. A discussion on the meaning of manifestation (التمثل) has already preceded in the commentary on Surah Maryam.

Allah, the Glorified, has confirmed the aforementioned meaning of manifestation in His saying regarding the story of the Messiah and Mary: فَأَرْسَلْنا إِلَيْها رُوحَنا فَتَمَثَّلَ لَها بَشَراً سَوِيًّا "Then We sent to her Our Spirit, and he manifested himself to her as a well-proportioned human" (19:17), and its commentary has already passed.

As for what is common on the tongues—that an angel is a subtle body (جسم لطيف) that can take different shapes except for the dog and the pig, and that the jinn is a subtle body that can take different shapes including the dog and the pig—it is something for which there is no proof from reason (عقل) nor from transmission (نقل) from the Book or the established Sunnah. As for what some have claimed regarding the consensus (إجماع) of the Muslims on this, in addition to it being contested, there is no proof for its authority (حجيته) in such doctrinal matters (المسائل الاعتقادية).

Ayahs

مَّا يَفْتَحِ ٱللَّهُ لِلنَّاسِ مِن رَّحْمَةٍ فَلَا مُمْسِكَ لَهَا ۖ وَمَا يُمْسِكْ فَلَا مُرْسِلَ لَهُۥ مِنۢ بَعْدِهِۦ ۚ وَهُوَ ٱلْعَزِيزُ ٱلْحَكِيمُ (٢)
Whatever mercy Allah unfolds for the people, no one can withhold it; and whatever He withholds no one can release it except Him, and He is the All-mighty, the All-wise. (2)
يَـٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلنَّاسُ ٱذْكُرُوا۟ نِعْمَتَ ٱللَّهِ عَلَيْكُمْ ۚ هَلْ مِنْ خَـٰلِقٍ غَيْرُ ٱللَّهِ يَرْزُقُكُم مِّنَ ٱلسَّمَآءِ وَٱلْأَرْضِ ۚ لَآ إِلَـٰهَ إِلَّا هُوَ ۖ فَأَنَّىٰ تُؤْفَكُونَ (٣)
O mankind! Remember Allah’s blessing upon you! Is there any creator other than Allah who provides for you from the sky and the earth? There is no god except Him. So where do you stray? (3)
وَإِن يُكَذِّبُوكَ فَقَدْ كُذِّبَتْ رُسُلٌ مِّن قَبْلِكَ ۚ وَإِلَى ٱللَّهِ تُرْجَعُ ٱلْأُمُورُ (٤)
If they impugn you, certainly [other] apostles were impugned before you, and all matters are returned to Allah. (4)
يَـٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلنَّاسُ إِنَّ وَعْدَ ٱللَّهِ حَقٌّ ۖ فَلَا تَغُرَّنَّكُمُ ٱلْحَيَوٰةُ ٱلدُّنْيَا ۖ وَلَا يَغُرَّنَّكُم بِٱللَّهِ ٱلْغَرُورُ (٥)
O mankind! Allah’s promise is indeed true. So do not let the life of the world deceive you, nor let the Deceiver deceive you concerning Allah. (5)
إِنَّ ٱلشَّيْطَـٰنَ لَكُمْ عَدُوٌّ فَٱتَّخِذُوهُ عَدُوًّا ۚ إِنَّمَا يَدْعُوا۟ حِزْبَهُۥ لِيَكُونُوا۟ مِنْ أَصْحَـٰبِ ٱلسَّعِيرِ (٦)
Satan is indeed your enemy, so treat him as an enemy. He only invites his confederates so that they may be among the inmates of the Blaze. (6)
ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ لَهُمْ عَذَابٌ شَدِيدٌ ۖ وَٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ وَعَمِلُوا۟ ٱلصَّـٰلِحَـٰتِ لَهُم مَّغْفِرَةٌ وَأَجْرٌ كَبِيرٌ (٧)
There is a severe punishment for the faithless; but for those who have faith and do righteous deeds, there will be forgiveness and a great reward. (7)
أَفَمَن زُيِّنَ لَهُۥ سُوٓءُ عَمَلِهِۦ فَرَءَاهُ حَسَنًا ۖ فَإِنَّ ٱللَّهَ يُضِلُّ مَن يَشَآءُ وَيَهْدِى مَن يَشَآءُ ۖ فَلَا تَذْهَبْ نَفْسُكَ عَلَيْهِمْ حَسَرَٰتٍ ۚ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ عَلِيمٌۢ بِمَا يَصْنَعُونَ (٨)
Is someone the evil of whose conduct is presented as decorous to him, so he regards it as good.... Indeed Allah leads astray whomever He wishes, and guides whomever He wishes. So do not fret yourself to death regretting for them. Indeed Allah knows best what they do. (8)

(Commentary)

After the author alluded to the angels, who are intermediaries in the arrival of blessings to the creation, he now points toward the blessings themselves in a universal manner. He mentions that all blessings are from Allah, the Glorified, and none other; for He is the Provider (الرازق) who has no partner in this regard. He then uses the attribute of providing (الرازقية) as an argument for His Lordship (الربوبية), and subsequently for the Resurrection (المعاد), asserting that His promise of the rising, the punishment of the disbelievers, and the forgiveness of the righteous believers is the truth. Within these verses is also a consolation for the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله).

His saying, the Exalted: مَا يَفْتَحِ اللَّهُ لِلنَّاسِ مِنْ رَحْمَةٍ فَلَا مُمْسِكَ لَهَا وَمَا يُمْسِكْ فَلَا مُرْسِلَ لَهُ مِنْ بَعْدِهِ — "Whatever mercy Allah unfolds for the people, no one can withhold it; and whatever He withholds, no one can release it after Him" (35:2). The meaning is that whatever blessing Allah grants to people—which is sustenance (الرزق)—there is none to prevent it.

And whatever He prevents, there is none to grant it. The apparent requirement of the context would have been to say: "Whatever Allah sends (يرسل) to the people...", just as He used the term "release/sending" (الإرسال) in the second clause. However, He shifted from "sending" to "unfolding/opening" (الفتح) because it occurs repeatedly in His speech that His mercy has treasuries (خزائن), such as His saying: أَمْ عِنْدَهُمْ خَزَائِنُ رَحْمَةِ رَبِّكَ الْعَزِيزِ الْوَهَّابِ — "Do they possess the treasuries of the mercy of your Lord, the All-mighty, the All-munificent?" (38:9), and His saying: قُلْ لَوْ أَنْتُمْ تَمْلِكُونَ خَزَائِنَ رَحْمَةِ رَبِّي إِذًا لَأَمْسَكْتُمْ خَشْيَةَ الْإِنْفَاقِ — "Say, ‘If you possessed the treasuries of my Lord’s mercy, you would surely withhold them for fear of spending’" (17:100). The expression of "opening" (الفتح) is more appropriate than "sending" when referring to treasuries. Thus, there is an indication that the mercy granted to people is stored in treasuries that encompass them; their attaining it depends on nothing but its being opened, without any additional burden.

Sustenance (الرزق), which is the blessing (النعمة), has been expressed as mercy (الرحمة) to demonstrate that His bestowing of these blessings arises from pure mercy, without any expectation of a benefit returning to Him or a perfection by which He might complete Himself.

And His saying: وَمَا يُمْسِكْ فَلَا مُرْسِلَ لَهُ مِنْ بَعْدِهِ — "and whatever He withholds, no one can release it after Him." That is, whatever He prevents of mercy, there is no releaser for it besides Him. In the expression "after Him" (من بعده), there is an indication that He, the Exalted, is the First in withholding just as He is the First in giving.

And His saying: وَهُوَ الْعَزِيزُ الْحَكِيمُ — "and He is the All-mighty, the All-wise." This is an affirmation of the ruling mentioned in the noble verse through these two noble Names. Because He, the Exalted, is the All-mighty (العزيز), He is never overcome when He gives; thus, no withholder can withhold from Him. And when He withholds, no giver can give. Furthermore, He, the Exalted, is the All-wise (الحكيم); when He gives, He gives out of wisdom (حكمة) and purpose (مصلحة), and when He withholds, He withholds out of wisdom and purpose. In short, there is no giver but Allah and no withholder but He, and His withholding and giving are based on wisdom.

His saying, the Exalted: يَا أَيُّهَا النَّاسُ اذْكُرُوا نِعْمَتَ اللَّهِ عَلَيْكُمْ هَلْ مِنْ خَالِقٍ غَيْرُ اللَّهِ يَرْزُقُكُمْ مِنَ السَّمَاءِ وَالْأَرْضِ — "O mankind! Remember Allah’s blessing upon you! Is there any creator other than Allah who provides for you from the sky and the earth?" (35:3). Since the previous verse established that giving and withholding belong to Allah, the Glorified, and no one shares that with Him, He uses that in this verse as an argument for His uniqueness in Lordship (الربوبية).

The formulation of the argument is that a deity is only a worshipped deity because of his Lordship, which is the faculty of managing the affairs (تدبير أمر) of people and others. The one who possesses the management of affairs through these blessings—in which people and others fluctuate and by which they are sustained—is Allah, the Glorified, to the exclusion of the other deities they have taken. This is because He, the Glorified, is the one who created them, while the others did not; and creation (الخلق) is inseparable from management (التدبير) and never departs from it. Thus, He, the Glorified, is your God; there is no god but He, because He is your Lord who manages your affairs through these blessings in which you fluctuate. He is only a managing Lord through these blessings because He is their Creator and the Creator of the system (النظام) that governs them.

By this, it becomes clear that the "people" (الناس) addressed are the idolaters and others who have taken a partner with Allah.

And His saying: اذْكُرُوا نِعْمَتَ اللَّهِ عَلَيْكُمْ — "Remember Allah’s blessing upon you." What is meant by remembrance (الذكر) here is the opposite of forgetfulness (النسيان), not merely verbal remembrance.

And His saying: هَلْ مِنْ خَالِقٍ غَيْرُ اللَّهِ يَرْزُقُكُمْ مِنَ السَّمَاءِ وَالْأَرْضِ — "Is there any creator other than Allah who provides for you from the sky and the earth?" Sustenance (الرزق) is that which aids in the preservation of existence. Its source is the sky, by means of rays, rain, and other things; and the earth, by means of plants, animals, and other things.

By this, it also becomes clear that there is a subtle conciseness in the verse. Mercy (الرحمة) in the previous verse was replaced by blessing (النعمة) in this verse first, and then blessing was replaced by sustenance (الرزق) second. The requirement of the context of the two verses would have been to say: "Is there any provider (رازق)..." or "Is there any benefactor (منعم)..." or "Is there any merciful one (راحم)..." but instead, it was replaced by His saying: هَلْ مِنْ خَالِقٍ — "Is there any creator...". This serves as an allusion to a second proof that cuts off all dispute. For they [the polytheists] believed that the management of the world belonged to their deities by Allah's permission. Had it been said: "Is there any provider or benefactor other than Allah?", the dispute would not have been settled, as they could have said: "Yes, our deities, through the delegation of management from Allah to them." However, when it was said: "Is there any creator...", the description pointed out that the Provider and Manager is the Creator of the sustenance and none other. Thus, the dispute was severed, and they could only respond by denying any creator other than Allah who provides for them from the sky and the earth.

And His saying: لَا إِلَهَ إِلَّا هُوَ — "There is no god but He." This is a parenthetical statement of monotheism (التوحيد) that serves to glorify Him, similar to His saying: وَقَالُوا اتَّخَذَ اللَّهُ وَلَدًا سُبْحَانَهُ — "And they say, ‘Allah has taken a son.’ Immaculate is He!" (2:116).

That is, there is no deity truly worthy of worship except Him, because the one entitled to worship is the one who bestows blessings upon you and provides for you, and that is none but Allah.

And His saying: فَأَنَّى تُؤْفَكُونَ — "So where are you being led astray?" This is a reproach branching from the preceding proof. That is: if the matter is as such and you acknowledge it, then until when will you be turned away from the truth toward falsehood, and from monotheism toward polytheism (الإشراك)?

Regarding the grammatical analysis of the verse, namely His saying: هَلْ مِنْ خَالِقٍ غَيْرُ اللَّهِ — "Is there any creator other than Allah...", there have been long disputes among the grammarians. What suits the aforementioned establishment of the proof is that "min" (مِنْ) is extra (زائدة) for the sake of universality (التعميم). His saying: غَيْرُ اللَّهِ — "other than Allah" is an adjective for "creator" (خالق) following its grammatical place (المحل). Likewise, His saying: يَرْزُقُكُمْ — "who provides for you...". And "from a creator" (مِنْ خَالِقٍ) is a subject (مبتدأ) whose predicate (الخبر) is omitted, being "exists" (موجود). His saying: لَا إِلَهَ إِلَّا هُوَ — "There is no god but He" is a parenthesis, and His saying: فَأَنَّى تُؤْفَكُونَ — "So where are you being led astray?" is a derivation from what preceded it.

His saying, the Exalted: وَإِنْ يُكَذِّبُوكَ فَقَدْ كُذِّبَتْ رُسُلٌ مِنْ قَبْلِكَ وَإِلَى اللَّهِ تُرْجَعُ الْأُمُورُ — "If they impugn you, then messengers were already impugned before you, and to Allah all matters are returned" (35:4). This is a consolation for the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله). That is: if they deny you after hearing these radiant proofs, do not grieve, for this is not unprecedented; messengers before you were denied by their nations and peoples. To Allah all matters are returned, and He will recompense them as they deserve for their denial of the truth after its manifestation; they cannot frustrate Him by their denial.

From here it becomes clear that His saying: فَقَدْ كُذِّبَتْ رُسُلٌ مِنْ قَبْلِكَ — "then messengers were already impugned before you" is an instance of placing the cause in the position of the effect, and that His saying: وَإِلَى اللَّهِ تُرْجَعُ الْأُمُورُ — "and to Allah all matters are returned" is coordinated with His saying: "then messengers were already impugned...".

His saying, the Exalted: يَا أَيُّهَا النَّاسُ إِنَّ وَعْدَ اللَّهِ حَقٌّ فَلَا تَغُرَّنَّكُمُ الْحَيَاةُ الدُّنْيَا وَلَا يَغُرَّنَّكُمْ بِاللَّهِ الْغَرُورُ — "O mankind! Allah’s promise is indeed true. So do not let the life of the world deceive you, nor let the Deceiver deceive you concerning Allah" (35:5). This is a general address to mankind reminding them of the Resurrection (المعاد), just as the previous general address reminded them of His uniqueness in Lordship and Divinity (الألوهية).

So His saying: إِنَّ وَعْدَ اللَّهِ حَقٌّ — "Allah’s promise is indeed true." That is, His promise that He will resurrect you and recompense every worker for his deed, whether good or evil, is "true" (حق), meaning established and certain to occur. He has explicitly stated this promise in His upcoming saying: الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا لَهُمْ عَذَابٌ شَدِيدٌ وَالَّذِينَ آمَنُوا وَعَمِلُوا الصَّالِحَاتِ لَهُمْ مَغْفِرَةٌ وَأَجْرٌ كَبِيرٌ — "For the faithless there is a severe punishment, and for those who have faith and do righteous deeds there is forgiveness and a great reward" (35:7).

And His saying: فَلَا تَغُرَّنَّكُمُ الْحَيَاةُ الدُّنْيَا — "So do not let the life of the world deceive you." Although the prohibition is directed toward the worldly life in form, it is in reality directed toward them. The meaning is: if Allah's promise is true, then do not be deceived by the worldly life through preoccupation with its adornment and being diverted by its pleasures and amusements that make you forget the Day of Reckoning, nor by being engrossed in seeking it and turning away from the truth.

And His saying: وَلَا يَغُرَّنَّكُمْ بِاللَّهِ الْغَرُورُ — "nor let the Deceiver deceive you concerning Allah." The word "Deceiver" (الغرور) with a fatha on the ghayn is an intensive form (صيغة مبالغة) of deception (الغرور) with a damma, referring to one who is excessive in deceiving and whose habit it is. It is apparent—as has been said—that what is meant by it is Satan (الشيطان). This is supported by the justification given in the following verse: إِنَّ الشَّيْطَانَ لَكُمْ عَدُوٌّ — "Indeed Satan is your enemy..." (35:6).

The meaning of his deceiving them "concerning Allah" is his directing their attention sometimes to the manifestations of His forbearance (حلم) and pardon (عفو), and at other times to the manifestations of His trial (ابتلاء), gradual leading to ruin (استدراج), and scheme (كيد). Thus, they see that preoccupation with the world, forgetting the Hereafter, and turning away from the truth and reality does not result in immediate punishment or entail accountability. They see that the children of the world, the more they persist in their pursuit, plunge into their heedlessness, and immerse themselves in sins, the more their living becomes pleasant, their life comfortable, and their status and honor among people increase. At that point, Satan casts into their hearts that there is no nobility except in advancing in the worldly life, and no news of what lies beyond it; and that the promise and threat contained in the true call, and the resurrection, reckoning, Paradise, and Fire reported by prophecy, are nothing but myths.

Thus, what is meant by Satan's deceiving man "concerning Allah" is man's being deceived by the way Allah treats man despite his heedlessness and injustice.

It has also been said that "the Deceiver" (الغرور) refers to the world itself which deceives man, and that His saying: وَلَا يَغُرَّنَّكُمْ بِاللَّهِ الْغَرُورُ is an emphasis of His saying: فَلَا تَغُرَّنَّكُمُ الْحَيَاةُ الدُّنْيَا by repeating its meaning.

His saying, the Exalted: إِنَّ الشَّيْطَانَ لَكُمْ عَدُوٌّ فَاتَّخِذُوهُ عَدُوًّا — "Indeed Satan is your enemy; so treat him as an enemy" (35:6). This is a justification for the preceding prohibition in His saying: وَلَا يَغُرَّنَّكُمْ بِاللَّهِ الْغَرُورُ. What is meant by Satan's enmity is that he has no concern except to seduce man and deprive him of the happiness of life and a good end. What is meant by "treating Satan as an enemy" is to avoid following his call to falsehood and not to obey him in what he suggests through his whisperings (وساوس) and allurements (تسويلات). For this reason, He justified his enmity by saying: إِنَّمَا يَدْعُو حِزْبَهُ — "He only invites his confederates...".

So His saying: إِنَّمَا يَدْعُو حِزْبَهُ لِيَكُونُوا مِنْ أَصْحابِ السَّعِيرِ — "He only invites his confederates that they may be among the inmates of the Blaze" is in the position of justifying what preceded it. A "confederacy" (الحزب) is a group of people united by a single purpose. The "lam" in "that they may be" (ليكونوا) is for causality (التعليل); thus, their being among the inmates of the Blaze is the final cause (علة غائية) of his invitation. The "Blaze" (السعير) is the kindled fire, and it is one of the names of Hell (جهنم) in the Quran.

His saying, the Exalted: الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا لَهُمْ عَذَابٌ شَدِيدٌ وَالَّذِينَ آمَنُوا وَعَمِلُوا الصَّالِحَاتِ لَهُمْ مَغْفِرَةٌ وَأَجْرٌ كَبِيرٌ — "For the faithless there is a severe punishment, and for those who have faith and do righteous deeds there is forgiveness and a great reward" (35:7). This is the true promise mentioned by Allah, the Glorified. The indefiniteness (تنكير) of "punishment" (عذاب) is to indicate its magnitude (التفخيم), and also that they have different levels (دركات) and ranks of punishment according to their disbelief and wickedness; thus, ambiguity is more appropriate. A similar two-fold reasoning applies to His saying: "forgiveness and a reward" (مغفرة وأجر).

His saying, the Exalted: أَفَمَنْ زُيِّنَ لَهُ سُوءُ عَمَلِهِ فَرَآهُ حَسَنًا فَإِنَّ اللَّهَ يُضِلُّ مَنْ يَشَاءُ وَيَهْدِي مَنْ يَشَاءُ — "Is he the evil of whose deeds is presented as decorous to him, so he regards it as good [like one who is guided]? Indeed Allah leads astray whomever He wishes and guides whomever He wishes" (35:8). This is an affirmation and explanation of the division contained in the previous verse—namely, the division of people into a disbeliever who has a severe punishment and a believer who does righteous deeds and has forgiveness and a great reward. The meaning is that the two are not equal, nor is the outcome of their affairs equal.

So His saying: أَفَمَنْ زُيِّنَ لَهُ سُوءُ عَمَلِهِ فَرَآهُ حَسَنًا — "Is he the evil of whose deeds is presented as decorous to him, so he regards it as good..." is a subject (مبتدأ) whose predicate (خبر) is omitted, i.e., "...like one who is not so?". The "fa" (ف) is to branch the sentence from the meaning of the previous verse, and the interrogative is for denial (الإنكار). What is meant by "he the evil of whose deeds is presented as decorous to him, so he regards it as good" is the disbeliever. He points out by this that his understanding is inverted and his intellect is overcome, seeing his deed as other than what it truly is. The meaning is that he whose evil deed is beautified for him so that he sees it as good is not equal to the one who is not like that, but rather sees the evil as evil.

And His saying: فَإِنَّ اللَّهَ يُضِلُّ مَنْ يَشَاءُ وَيَهْدِي مَنْ يَشَاءُ — "Indeed Allah leads astray whomever He wishes and guides whomever He wishes" is a justification for the preceding denial in His saying: "Is he the evil of whose deeds...". That is: the disbeliever who is such, and the believer who is the opposite, are not equal because Allah leads one of them astray by His will—and that is the disbeliever who sees the evil as good—and He guides the other by His will—and that is the believer who does righteous deeds and sees the evil as evil.

This leading astray (الإضلال) is a leading astray by way of recompense (المجازاة) and is not an initial leading astray (إضلال ابتدائي); thus, there is no harm in attributing it to Allah, the Glorified.

In short, the difference between the disbeliever and the believer in their outcomes, according to the divine promise of punishment and mercy, is due to their difference in divine leading astray and guidance; and their difference in leading astray and guidance is due to their difference in seeing the evil as good or otherwise.

And His saying: فَلَا تَذْهَبْ نَفْسُكَ عَلَيْهِمْ حَسَرَاتٍ — "so do not let yourself be consumed by regrets for them." Regrets (الحسرات) is the plural of regret (حسرة), which is grief over what has passed and remorse for it. It is in the accusative case because it is an object of purpose (مفعول لأجله). What is meant by the "going of the soul" (ذهاب النفس) over them is its perishing because of the regrets arising from their lack of faith.

The sentence is branched from the preceding distinction. That is: if the two groups are different in terms of leading astray and guidance from Allah's side, then do not let your soul perish in regrets over them because they denied you and disbelieved in you. For Allah is the one who leads them astray as a recompense for their disbelief and their seeing the evil as good, and He is All-knowing of what they do; thus, the matter is not confused for Him, and He does not do with them except what is right, nor does He recompense them except with justice.

From here it becomes clear that His saying: إِنَّ اللَّهَ عَلِيمٌ بِمَا يَصْنَعُونَ — "Indeed Allah knows best what they do" is in the position of justifying His saying: فَلَا تَذْهَبْ نَفْسُكَ عَلَيْهِمْ حَسَرَاتٍ. It is not appropriate for the Messenger (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) to let his soul perish in regrets over them when they have gone astray and the word of punishment has become due against them, for Allah is the one who leads them astray because of their conduct, and He is All-knowing of what they do.

Ayahs

وَٱللَّهُ ٱلَّذِىٓ أَرْسَلَ ٱلرِّيَـٰحَ فَتُثِيرُ سَحَابًا فَسُقْنَـٰهُ إِلَىٰ بَلَدٍ مَّيِّتٍ فَأَحْيَيْنَا بِهِ ٱلْأَرْضَ بَعْدَ مَوْتِهَا ۚ كَذَٰلِكَ ٱلنُّشُورُ (٩)
It is Allah who sends the winds and they raise a cloud; then We drive it toward a dead land and with it revive the earth after its death. Likewise will be the resurrection [of the dead]. (9)
مَن كَانَ يُرِيدُ ٱلْعِزَّةَ فَلِلَّهِ ٱلْعِزَّةُ جَمِيعًا ۚ إِلَيْهِ يَصْعَدُ ٱلْكَلِمُ ٱلطَّيِّبُ وَٱلْعَمَلُ ٱلصَّـٰلِحُ يَرْفَعُهُۥ ۚ وَٱلَّذِينَ يَمْكُرُونَ ٱلسَّيِّـَٔاتِ لَهُمْ عَذَابٌ شَدِيدٌ ۖ وَمَكْرُ أُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ هُوَ يَبُورُ (١٠)
Whoever seeks honour [should know that] honour entirely belongs to Allah. To Him ascends the good word, and He elevates righteous conduct; as for those who devise evil schemes, there is a severe punishment for them, and their plotting shall come to naught. (10)
وَٱللَّهُ خَلَقَكُم مِّن تُرَابٍ ثُمَّ مِن نُّطْفَةٍ ثُمَّ جَعَلَكُمْ أَزْوَٰجًا ۚ وَمَا تَحْمِلُ مِنْ أُنثَىٰ وَلَا تَضَعُ إِلَّا بِعِلْمِهِۦ ۚ وَمَا يُعَمَّرُ مِن مُّعَمَّرٍ وَلَا يُنقَصُ مِنْ عُمُرِهِۦٓ إِلَّا فِى كِتَـٰبٍ ۚ إِنَّ ذَٰلِكَ عَلَى ٱللَّهِ يَسِيرٌ (١١)
Allah created you from dust, then from a drop of [seminal] fluid, then He made you mates. No female conceives or delivers except with His knowledge, and no elderly person advances in years, nor is anything diminished of his life, but it is [recorded] in a Book. That is indeed easy for Allah. (11)
وَمَا يَسْتَوِى ٱلْبَحْرَانِ هَـٰذَا عَذْبٌ فُرَاتٌ سَآئِغٌ شَرَابُهُۥ وَهَـٰذَا مِلْحٌ أُجَاجٌ ۖ وَمِن كُلٍّ تَأْكُلُونَ لَحْمًا طَرِيًّا وَتَسْتَخْرِجُونَ حِلْيَةً تَلْبَسُونَهَا ۖ وَتَرَى ٱلْفُلْكَ فِيهِ مَوَاخِرَ لِتَبْتَغُوا۟ مِن فَضْلِهِۦ وَلَعَلَّكُمْ تَشْكُرُونَ (١٢)
Not alike are the two seas: this one sweet and agreeable, pleasant to drink, and that one briny and bitter, and from each you eat fresh meat and obtain ornaments, which you wear. And you see the ships plowing through them, that you may seek of His bounty, and so that you may give thanks. (12)
يُولِجُ ٱلَّيْلَ فِى ٱلنَّهَارِ وَيُولِجُ ٱلنَّهَارَ فِى ٱلَّيْلِ وَسَخَّرَ ٱلشَّمْسَ وَٱلْقَمَرَ كُلٌّ يَجْرِى لِأَجَلٍ مُّسَمًّى ۚ ذَٰلِكُمُ ٱللَّهُ رَبُّكُمْ لَهُ ٱلْمُلْكُ ۚ وَٱلَّذِينَ تَدْعُونَ مِن دُونِهِۦ مَا يَمْلِكُونَ مِن قِطْمِيرٍ (١٣)
He makes the night pass into the day and makes the day pass into the night, and He has disposed the sun and the moon, each moving for a specified term. That is Allah, your Lord; to Him belongs all sovereignty. As for those whom you invoke besides Him, they do not control so much as the husk of a date stone. (13)
إِن تَدْعُوهُمْ لَا يَسْمَعُوا۟ دُعَآءَكُمْ وَلَوْ سَمِعُوا۟ مَا ٱسْتَجَابُوا۟ لَكُمْ ۖ وَيَوْمَ ٱلْقِيَـٰمَةِ يَكْفُرُونَ بِشِرْكِكُمْ ۚ وَلَا يُنَبِّئُكَ مِثْلُ خَبِيرٍ (١٤)
If you invoke them they will not hear your invocation, and even if they heard they cannot respond to you, and on the Day of Resurrection they will forswear your polytheism, and none can inform you like the One who is all-aware. (14)

(Commentary)

These are arguments for His oneness in His divinity (الألوهية), following a series of celestial and terrestrial blessings that man enjoys, for which there is no creator or regulator (مدبر) except Allah, the Glorified. Within them is also an allusion to the Resurrection (البعث).

His saying, the Exalted: وَاللهُ الَّذِي أَرْسَلَ الرِّياحَ فَتُثِيرُ سَحاباً فَسُقْناهُ إِلى بَلَدٍ مَيِّتٍ — It is Allah who sends the winds and they stir up a cloud, then We drive it to a dead land [35:9], etc.

The focus in this context is on the actual occurrence of rainfall and the subsequent growth of vegetation. For this reason, He said: اللهُ الَّذِي أَرْسَلَ الرِّياحَ (Allah is He who sent the winds), using the past tense. This differs from the phrasing in Surah al-Rum: اللهُ الَّذِي يُرْسِلُ الرِّياحَ فَتُثِيرُ سَحاباً — It is Allah who sends the winds and they stir up a cloud [30:48], where the present tense is used.

His saying: فَتُثِيرُ سَحاباً (and they stir up a cloud) is conjoined to أَرْسَلَ (sent). The pronoun refers to the winds. The use of the present tense (المضارع) is to depict the past state (حكاية الحال الماضية). Stirring (الإثارة) is the causative form (إفعال) from the root "the dust stirred" (ثار الغبار), meaning it spread and rose up.

His saying: فَسُقْناهُ إِلى بَلَدٍ مَيِّتٍ (then We drive it to a dead land) means to a land with no vegetation. فَأَحْيَيْنا بِهِ الْأَرْضَ بَعْدَ مَوْتِها — then We revive the earth with it after its death [35:9]; that is, We caused plants to grow in it after they were non-existent. Attributing life (الإحياء) to the earth, though metaphorical (مجازية), is literal (حقيقية) when attributed to the plants. The functions of plants—such as nutrition, growth, reproduction, and related processes—are vital activities (أعمال حيوية) emanating from the principle of life (أصل الحياة).

Therefore, He likened the Resurrection (البعث) and the reviving of the dead after their death to the reviving of the earth after its death—meaning the growth of plants after their cessation and dormancy in winter—saying: كَذلِكَ النُّشُورُ — likewise is the resurrection [35:9]. The resurrection (النشور) is the spreading out of the dead on the Day of Judgment after their revival and emergence from the graves.

In His saying: فَسُقْناهُ إِلى بَلَدٍ مَيِّتٍ (then We drive it to a dead land), there is a transition (التفات) from the third person (الغيبة) to the first person plural (التكلم مع الغير). In His saying: وَاللهُ الَّذِي أَرْسَلَ (And it is Allah who sent), He is described in the third person, while in فَسُقْناهُ (then We drive it), He is described in the first person plural. Perhaps the subtle point (النكتة) here is that when He said: وَاللهُ الَّذِي أَرْسَلَ الرِّياحَ, He took the attribute of the third person, and the act of "sending" followed it, as the act of an absent one is itself absent. Then, when He said: فَتُثِيرُ سَحاباً in the manner of depicting a past state, the listener becomes as if he is seeing the act and witnessing the winds as they stir the clouds and spread them in the atmosphere. Thus, it is as if he sees the One who sends the winds, because witnessing the act is almost inseparable from witnessing the Agent (الفاعل). When the Exalted appeared in the attribute of presence (الحضور), He changed the style of His speech from the third person to the first person, choosing the plural form to indicate majesty (العظمة).

His saying: فَأَحْيَيْنا بِهِ الْأَرْضَ (then We revived the earth with it), and He did not say "then We revived it" (فأحييناه), though it would have sufficed. Similarly, His saying: بَعْدَ مَوْتِها (after its death), though it could have been omitted based on what preceded it, was stated to adopt an explicit declaration (صريح القول) about which there is no doubt.

His saying, the Exalted: مَنْ كانَ يُرِيدُ الْعِزَّةَ فَلِلَّهِ الْعِزَّةُ جَمِيعاً — Whoever seeks honour—to Allah belongs all honour [35:10]. Al-Raghib said in al-Mufradat: "Honour (العزة) is a state that prevents a person from being overcome, derived from their saying: 'hard ground' (أرض عزاز), meaning solid." The Exalted said: أَيَبْتَغُونَ عِنْدَهُمُ الْعِزَّةَ فَإِنَّ الْعِزَّةُ لِلَّهِ جَمِيعاً — Do they seek honour with them? Indeed, all honour belongs to Allah [4:139]. (End of quote).

Thus, solidity (الصلابة) is the root meaning of honour (العزة). It was then expanded so that "the Mighty" (العزيز) is used for one who subdues and is not subdued, as in His saying: يا أَيُّهَا الْعَزِيزُ مَسَّنا — O Emir! Distress has befallen us [12:88]. Likewise, honour (العزة) can mean victory (الغلبة), as the Exalted said: وَعَزَّنِي فِي الْخِطابِ — and he has got the better of me in speech [38:23]. Honour (العزة) can also mean scarcity and being difficult to attain, as the Exalted said: وَإِنَّهُ لَكِتابٌ عَزِيزٌ — and indeed it is an august Book [41:41]. It can mean absolute difficulty, as the Exalted said: عَزِيزٌ عَلَيْهِ ما عَنِتُّمْ — grievous to him is your distress [9:128]. It can also mean pride (الأنفة) and zeal (الحمية), as the Exalted said: بَلِ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا فِي عِزَّةٍ وَشِقاقٍ — Rather the faithless are in [the grip of] pride and defiance [38:2], among other meanings.

Furthermore, honour (العزة) in the sense of a thing being subduing and not subdued, or victorious and not defeated, belongs in its true sense exclusively to Allah, the Mighty and Majestic. For anyone other than the Exalted is poor in his essence (ذاته) and lowly in himself, possessing nothing for himself unless Allah has mercy on him and grants him some honour, as He did for the believers. The Exalted said: وَلِلَّهِ الْعِزَّةُ وَلِرَسُولِهِ وَلِرَسُولِهِ وَلِلْمُؤْمِنِينَ — Yet all might belongs to Allah and His Apostle and the faithful [63:8].

From this, it becomes clear that His saying: مَنْ كانَ يُرِيدُ الْعِزَّةَ فَلِلَّهِ الْعِزَّةُ جَمِيعاً is not intended to state that honour is so exclusive to Allah that no one else can attain it, or that whoever seeks it has sought the impossible. Rather, the meaning is: whoever wants honour, let him seek it from the Exalted, because all honour belongs to Him and is not found inherently (بالذات) with anyone else.

Thus, placing His saying: فَلِلَّهِ الْعِزَّةُ جَمِيعاً as the response to the condition (جزاء الشرط) is an instance of placing the cause (السبب) in the position of the effect (المسبب), which is seeking it from Him—that is, acquiring it from Him through servitude (العبودية), which is only achieved through faith and righteous deeds (العمل الصالح).

His saying, the Exalted: إِلَيْهِ يَصْعَدُ الْكَلِمُ الطَّيِّبُ وَالْعَمَلُ الصَّالِحُ يَرْفَعُهُ — To Him ascends the good word, and He elevates righteous conduct [35:10]. The word (الكلم) is—as has been said—a collective generic noun (اسم جنس جمعي) that can be treated as masculine or feminine. It is said in al-Majma': "al-Kalim (الكلم) is the plural of kalimah (كلمة). It is said: 'this is kalim' (هذا كلم) and 'these are kalim' (هذه كلم), so it is treated as both masculine and feminine. Every plural that differs from its singular only by the letter ha (الهاء) may be treated as masculine or feminine." (End of quote).

In any case, what is meant by "the word" (الكلم) is that which conveys a complete linguistic meaning, as evidenced by its description as "good" (الطيب). The goodness of a word is its suitability to the soul of its listener and speaker, such that the soul expands because of it, finds pleasure in it, and is perfected by it. This only occurs when it conveys a true meaning (معنى حقا) containing the soul's felicity and success.

From this, it appears that what is meant is not the mere phonetic utterance, but rather the good meaning it carries. Thus, it refers to true beliefs (الاعتقادات الحقة) through which man finds felicity by submitting to them and basing his actions upon them. The most certain of these is the word of monotheism (كلمة التوحيد) — the absolute oneness of Allah in His essence, attributes, and acts — to which all other true beliefs return. This is what is encompassed by His saying, the Exalted: أَلَمْ تَرَ كَيْفَ ضَرَبَ اللهُ مَثَلاً كَلِمَةً طَيِّبةً كَشَجَرَةٍ طَيِّبَةٍ أَصْلُها ثابِتٌ وَفَرْعُها فِي السَّماءِ تُؤْتِي أُكُلَها كُلَّ حِينٍ بِإِذْنِ رَبِّها — Have you not regarded how Allah draws a parable? A good word is like a good tree: its roots are steady and its branches are in the sky. It gives its fruit every season by the leave of its Lord [14:24-25]. Calling a belief a "saying" or a "word" is a common practice among them.

The ascent (صعود) of the good word to Him, the Exalted, is its drawing near to Him by way of elevation, for He is the Most High (العلي الأعلى), the Exalted in Rank (رفيع الدرجات). Since belief is an attribute subsisting in the believer, its drawing near to Him is the drawing near of the believer to Him. Some have interpreted the ascent of the good word as His acceptance (القبول) of it, which is one of the implications of the meaning.

Furthermore, when belief and faith are true and sincere within the soul, they are confirmed by action and not belied by it; that is, actions proceed in accordance with them. Action is one of the branches of knowledge and its inseparable effects. Whenever an action is repeated, the belief increases in firmness and clarity, and its influence grows stronger. Thus, righteous deeds (العمل الصالح)—which are deeds worthy of acceptance, characterized by the offering of servitude and sincerity for His noble sake—assist the true belief in producing its effect, which is the ascent to Him. This is what is referred to by "elevating" (الرفع); thus, righteous conduct elevates the good word.

It has become clear from what has passed the meaning of His saying: إِلَيْهِ يَصْعَدُ الْكَلِمُ الطَّيِّبُ وَالْعَمَلُ الصَّالِحُ يَرْفَعُهُ. The pronoun in إِلَيْهِ (to Him) refers to Allah, the Glorified. The "good word" refers to true belief, such as monotheism (التوحيد). Its "ascent" is its drawing near to Him. "Righteous conduct" refers to what is in accordance with and suitable to the true belief. The agent (الفاعل) in يَرْفَعُهُ (elevates it) is a hidden pronoun referring to "righteous conduct," and the object pronoun refers to the "good word."

Others have different views regarding this verse:

It has been said that the ascent of the good word means its acceptance and the granting of reward for it, as previously mentioned. It has also been said that it refers to the ascent of the angels with the recorded faith and acts of obedience to Allah, the Glorified. It has been said that it means their ascent with it to the heavens, so the ascent to the heavens is metaphorically called an ascent to Allah.

It has been said that the agent of يَرْفَعُهُ is a pronoun returning to the "good word," and the object pronoun refers to "righteous conduct." The meaning would be that the good word elevates righteous conduct; i.e., righteous conduct is of no benefit unless it proceeds from monotheism. It has also been said that the agent of يَرْفَعُهُ is a hidden pronoun returning to Him, the Exalted, meaning: Allah elevates righteous conduct.

All of these views are not without some far-fetchedness (بعد), and the meaning that first comes to mind is what we previously stated.

His saying, the Exalted: وَالَّذِينَ يَمْكُرُونَ السَّيِّئاتِ لَهُمْ عَذابٌ شَدِيدٌ وَمَكْرُ أُولئِكَ هُوَ يَبُورُ — As for those who plot evil schemes, there is a severe punishment for them, and their plotting will come to naught [35:10]. They mentioned that "evil" (السَّيِّئاتِ) is an adjective standing in place of an omitted noun, which is "plots" (المكرات). The use of the demonstrative pronoun (اسم الإشارة) instead of a simple pronoun in مَكْرُ أُولئِكَ (the plotting of those) is to indicate that they are distinct and not mixed with others. The meaning is: those who plot evil plots shall have a severe punishment, and the plotting of those plotters shall perish (يبور) and be destroyed, leaving no living trace that contains their felicity or honour.

It has become clear that "evil" refers to the types of plots and ruses that the polytheists (المشركون) take as means to gain honour. The verse is general (مطلقة). It has been said that it refers to the plots that the Quraysh devised against the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) in the Assembly Hall (دار الندوة) and elsewhere—to imprison, expel, or kill him. Allah turned their plot back upon them, brought them out to Badr, killed them, and cast them into the well (القليب), thus combining for them imprisonment, expulsion, and death. This is a fine interpretation, but the verse remains general.

The connection between the end of the verse—namely, His saying: إِلَيْهِ يَصْعَدُ to the end—and His saying: مَنْ كانَ يُرِيدُ الْعِزَّةَ فَلِلَّهِ الْعِزَّةُ جَمِيعاً is that the polytheists used to seek honour through their gods, as the Exalted said: وَاتَّخَذُوا مِنْ دُونِ اللهِ آلِهَةً لِيَكُونُوا لَهُمْ عِزًّا — They have taken gods besides Allah that they may be a [source of] might for them [19:81]. So Allah, the Glorified, called them to Himself while they were seeking honour, by reminding them that all honour belongs to Allah. The Exalted explained this by stating that monotheism (توحيده) ascends to Him and righteous conduct elevates it, so man acquires honour from the source of honour by drawing near to Him. As for those who plot every evil plot to acquire honour, they shall have a severe punishment, and their plotting is futile and perishing; it does not ascend to any place nor does it earn them any honour.

His saying, the Exalted: وَاللهُ خَلَقَكُمْ مِنْ تُرابٍ ثُمَّ مِنْ نُطْفَةٍ ثُمَّ جَعَلَكُمْ أَزْواجاً — Allah created you from dust, then from a drop of fluid, then He made you pairs [35:11], etc. The Exalted points to the creation of man, beginning his creation from dust, which is the remote origin (المبدأ البعيد) to which creation traces back, then from a drop of fluid (نطفة), which is the proximate origin (مبدأ قريب) to which creation is attached.

It has been said that "creating them from dust" refers to the creation of their father Adam from dust, as a thing is attributed to its root. It has been said that it refers to the creation of Adam himself. It has also been said that it refers to creating them collectively (إجماليا) from dust within the creation of Adam from dust, while the detailed creation (الخلق التفصيلي) is from a drop of fluid, as He said: ثُمَّ مِنْ نُطْفَةٍ.

The difference between these three views is that in the first, the attribution of creation from dust to them is by way of intellectual metaphor (المجاز العقلي). In the second, what is meant by "creating them" is the creation of Adam, and there is no metaphor in the attribution. In the third, what is meant is the creation of every individual from dust in reality without metaphor, except that it is a collective creation, not a detailed one; this distinguishes it from the first view we mentioned.

The first view can be supported by His saying, the Exalted: خَلَقَ الْإِنْسانَ مِنْ صَلْصالٍ كَالْفَخَّارِ — He created man from dry clay like in pottery [55:14]. The second by something like His saying: وَبَدأَ خَلْقَ الْإِنْسانِ مِنْ طِينٍ ثُمَّ جَعَلَ نَسْلَهُ مِنْ سُلالَةٍ مِنْ ماءٍ مَهِينٍ — and He began the creation of man from clay. Then He made his progeny from an extract of a base fluid [32:7-8]. The third by His saying: وَلَقَدْ خَلَقْناكُمْ ثُمَّ صَوَّرْناكُمْ ثُمَّ قُلْنا لِلْمَلائِكَةِ اسْجُدُوا لِآدَمَ — Certainly We created you, then We formed you, then We said to the angels, ‘Prostrate before Adam’ [7:11]. Each view has its merit.

His saying: ثُمَّ جَعَلَكُمْ أَزْواجاً (then He made you pairs) means males and females. It has been said: He ordained marriage (الزوجية) among you and paired some of you with others, but this is as you see (weak). It has also been said: He made you into types and nations, which is like the preceding view.

His saying: وَما تَحْمِلُ مِنْ أُنْثى وَلا تَضَعُ إِلَّا بِعِلْمِهِ — No female conceives or delivers except with His knowledge [35:11]. The word "any" (من) is extra (زائدة) to emphasize the negation. The preposition "with" (الباء) in بِعِلْمِهِ (with His knowledge) denotes accompaniment (المصاحبة), and it is a circumstantial qualifier (حال) for the acts of conceiving and delivering. The meaning is: no female conceives or delivers except that His knowledge accompanies her conceiving and delivering. Some mentioned it is a circumstantial qualifier for the agent (الفاعل), but that it being a qualifier for the acts of conceiving and delivering, or for their objects—the conceived and the delivered—is contrary to the apparent meaning, which is rejected.

His saying: وَما يُعَمَّرُ مِنْ مُعَمَّرٍ وَلا يُنْقَصُ مِنْ عُمُرِهِ إِلَّا فِي كِتابٍ — and no aged person is given [a long] life, nor is any part of his life curtailed, but it is in a Book [35:11]. That is, no one's life is extended or increased so that he becomes aged (معمرا), nor is any part of his life—meaning anyone's life—curtailed, except that it is in a Book.

His saying: وَما يُعَمَّرُ مِنْ مُعَمَّرٍ is like the saying: إِنِّي أَرانِي أَعْصِرُ خَمْراً — I saw myself pressing wine [12:36]. The word "aged" (معمر) is placed in the position of the pro-agent (نائب الفاعل), which is "anyone," with the consideration that after the act of aging is applied to him, he becomes aged; otherwise, "aging the aged" would be meaningless.

His saying: وَلا يُنْقَصُ مِنْ عُمُرِهِ (nor is any part of his life curtailed), the pronoun in عُمُرِهِ (his life) refers to "aged" (معمر) in consideration of its omitted described noun, which is "anyone." The meaning is: nor is the life of anyone curtailed. Otherwise, curtailing the life of one who is assumed to be aged would be a contradiction that violates the assumption.

His saying: إِلاَّ فِي كِتابٍ (but it is in a Book) refers to the Preserved Tablet (اللوح المحفوظ) which is not subject to change. It is written therein that so-and-so shall have his life increased by such an amount for such a reason, and so-and-so shall have his life curtailed by such an amount for such a reason. As for the Book of Effacement and Affirmation (كتاب المحو والإثبات), it is the locus of change. The context of the verse conveys the description of fixed knowledge (العلم الثابت). Regarding His saying: وَما يُعَمَّرُ مِنْ مُعَمَّرٍ وَلا يُنْقَصُ مِنْ عُمُرِهِ, they have other weak views that are not worth addressing.

His saying: إِنَّ ذلِكَ عَلَى اللهِ يَسِيرٌ — That is indeed easy for Allah [35:11]. This is a justification and confirmation of the description of man's creation and the manner of his origination and preservation mentioned in the verse. The meaning is that this precise and firm regulation (التدبير), which dominates universal and particular events and places everything in its proper place, is easy for Allah because He is Allah, the All-Knowing, the All-Powerful, who encompasses everything with His knowledge and power. Thus, He, the Exalted, is the Lord of man just as He is the Lord of everything.

His saying, the Exalted: وَما يَسْتَوِي الْبَحْرانِ هذا عَذْبٌ فُراتٌ سائِغٌ شَرابُهُ وَهذا مِلْحٌ أُجاجٌ — The two seas are not equal: this one is sweet, thirst-quenching, and pleasant to drink, and that one is salt and bitter [35:12], to the end of the verse. It is said: "sweet" (العذب) water is that which is good; "thirst-quenching" (الفرات) is water that breaks thirst or is cold, as in al-Majma'; "pleasant" (السائغ) is that which descends easily down the throat due to its sweetness; and "bitter" (الأجاج) is that which burns due to its saltiness or is acrid.

His saying: وَمِنْ كُلٍّ تَأْكُلُونَ لَحْماً طَرِيًّا وَتَسْتَخْرِجُونَ حِلْيَةً تَلْبَسُونَها — From each you eat fresh meat and extract ornaments that you wear [35:12]. "Fresh meat" (اللحم الطري) is tender and new, referring to the meat of fish, or fish and birds of the sea. The "ornaments" (الحلية) extracted from the sea are pearls, coral, and shells. The Exalted said: يَخْرُجُ مِنْهُمَا اللُّؤْلُؤُ وَالْمَرْجانُ — From them both emerge the pearl and the coral [55:22].

In the verse is a parable (تمثيل) for the believer and the disbeliever (الكافر) using the sweet and salt seas. It clarifies that the believer and the disbeliever are not equal in primordial perfection (الكمال الفطري), even if they share most human characteristics and their effects. The believer remains upon his original primordial nature (الفطرة), through which he attains the felicity of eternal life. The disbeliever has deviated from it, becoming shrouded in what the human nature does not find pleasant, and he will be punished for his deeds. Their likeness is that of two seas differing in sweetness and saltiness; they are different in terms of remaining upon the original nature of water—which is sweetness—or departing from it through saltiness, even if they share some beneficial effects. From each of them you eat fresh meat—which is the meat of fish and birds caught from the sea—and you extract ornaments that you wear, such as pearls, coral, and shells.

The apparent meaning of the verse is that the extracted ornaments are common to both the sweet sea and the salt sea. However, a group of commentators found this problematic, arguing that pearls and coral are only extracted from the salt sea, not the sweet one. They have provided various answers to this:

First, that the verse is intended to state the shared benefit of the two seas in general, even if some benefits are specific. It is as if it were said: "From each you benefit and profit, just as you eat fresh meat from both, and you extract ornaments from the salt sea that you wear, and you see the ships sailing through it."

Second, that He likened the believer and the disbeliever to the sweet and bitter waters, then He favored the bitter water over the disbeliever, in that there is some benefit in the bitter water while there is no benefit in the existence of the disbeliever. Thus, the verse follows the method of His saying: ثُمَّ قَسَتْ قُلُوبُكُمْ مِنْ بَعْدِ ذلِكَ فَهِيَ كَالْحِجارَةِ أَوْ أَشَدُّ قَسْوَةً — Then your hearts hardened after that; so they are like stones, or even harder in hardness [2:74], then He said: وَإِنَّ مِنَ الْحِجارَةِ لَما يَتَفَجَّرُ مِنْهُ الْأَنْهارُ — For indeed there are some stones from which rivers gush forth [2:74].

Third, that His saying: وَتَسْتَخْرِجُونَ حِلْيَةً تَلْبَسُونَها is part of the completion of the parable, meaning that although the two seas share some benefits, they differ in what is intended essentially (بالذات), because one of them has been mixed with something that removed it from the purity of its nature. Similarly, the believer and the disbeliever, though they may occasionally agree in some noble traits like courage and generosity, are different in what is fundamental, because one remains upon the purity of the original nature while the other does not.

Fourth, that there is nothing to prevent pearls from emerging from sweet waters even if we have not seen it; thus, the objection that ornaments are exclusive to salt water is rejected.

Fifth, rejecting the basis of the claim itself—that the verse وَما يَسْتَوِي الْبَحْرانِ etc., is a parable for the believer and the disbeliever. Rather, it occurs in the context of enumerating blessings to prove Lordship (الربوبية), like His previous saying: وَاللهُ الَّذِي أَرْسَلَ الرِّياحَ and His subsequent saying: يُولِجُ اللَّيْلَ فِي النَّهارِ etc. Thus, the verse is intended to explain the blessing of the sea, its variation in sweetness and saltiness, and the shared and specific benefits within them.

This view is supported by the fact that the counterpart of this verse in Surah al-Nahl occurs in the context of verses enumerating the blessings of Allah, the Glorified, which is His saying: وَهُوَ الَّذِي سَخَّرَ الْبَحْرَ لِتَأْكُلُوا مِنْهُ لَحْماً طَرِيًّا وَتَسْتَخْرِجُوا مِنْهُ حِلْيَةً تَلْبَسُونَها وَتَرَى الْفُلْكَ مَواخِرَ فِيهِ وَلِتَبْتَغُوا مِنْ فَضْلِهِ وَلَعَلَّكُمْ تَشْكُرُونَ — It is He who disposed the sea [for your benefit] that you may eat fresh meat from it, and extract from it ornaments which you wear, and you see the ships plowing through it, and that you may seek of His bounty, and that you may give thanks [16:14].
