Indeed Allah is the knower of the Unseen of the heavens and the earth. Indeed, He knows well what is in the breasts. (38)

(Commentary)

This section returns to mentioning other signs (آيات) of monotheism (التوحيد) — the absolute oneness of Allah in His essence, attributes, and acts — and transitions into a discourse regarding the Book and that it is the truth (الحق) revealed from Allah, the Exalted. The discourse in the previous section of verses had led to the mention of prophethood (النبوة) and the Book, where He said: إِنَّا أَرْسَلْنَاكَ بِالْحَقِّ بَشِيرًا وَنَذِيرًا — "Indeed We have sent you with the truth as a bearer of good news and as a warner" (35:24), and He said: جَاءَتْهُمْ رُسُلُهُمْ بِالْبَيِّنَاتِ وَبِالزُّبُرِ وَبِالْكِتَابِ الْمُنِيرِ — "their apostles brought them manifest proofs, and scriptures, and the illuminating Book" (35:25). Thus, it was appropriate to address the description of the Book and the effects that follow from it.

His saying, the Exalted: أَلَمْ تَرَ أَنَّ اللهَ أَنْزَلَ مِنَ السَّمَاءِ مَاءً فَأَخْرَجْنَا بِهِ ثَمَرَاتٍ مُخْتَلِفًا أَلْوَانُهَا — Have you not regarded that Allah sends down water from the sky, with which We bring forth fruits of varying colours? (35:27) etc. This is another proof for monotheism (التوحيد), which is that Allah, the Glorified, sends down water from the sky through rain, and it is the strongest factor (عامل) assisting in the emergence of fruits. If their emergence were merely according to the requirement of the nature (طبع) of this factor—which is one—then all of them would be of a single color. Thus, the variation in colors indicates the occurrence of divine governance (التدبير الإلهي).

The argument that their variation is contingent upon the variation of the influential factors (العوامل المؤثرة) within them—including the variation of the elements (العناصر) present in them in terms of type, quantity, and the specificity of their composition—is refuted. This is because the discussion then shifts to the variation of the elements themselves, which ultimately trace back to the common matter (المادة المشتركة) in which there is no variation. Therefore, the variation of the elements composed from it indicates another factor beyond matter that governs its affairs and directs it toward different ends.

It appears that what is meant by the variation in the colors of the fruits is the variation of their actual colors, which entails other variations in terms of taste, scent, and properties (الخواص). It has also been said that the variation in colors refers to the variation in species (الأنواع), as the term "color" (اللون) is often applied to species in the context of fruits and foods, as when it is said: "So-and-so presented various colors (ألوان) of food and fruit"; thus, it would be a form of metonymy (الكناية). His subsequent saying: وَمِنَ الْجِبَالِ جُدَدٌ بِيضٌ وَحُمْرٌ — "and in the mountains are stripes white and red" (35:27) provides some support for the first view.

In His saying: فَأَخْرَجْنَا بِهِ — "with which We bring forth," there is a transition (التفات) from the third person (الغيبة) to the first person (التكلم). It is said that this is for the sake of complete attention to the act, due to the wondrous craftsmanship (الصنع البديع) it contains, which informs of perfect power and wisdom.

A similar reasoning applies to His previous saying: إِنَّا أَرْسَلْنَاكَ بِالْحَقِّ بَشِيرًا وَنَذِيرًا — "Indeed We have sent you with the truth as a bearer of good news and as a warner" (35:24). As for what appeared in the previous verse: ثُمَّ أَخَذْتُ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا فَكَيْفَ كَانَ نَكِيرِ — "Then I seized those who were faithless, so how was My rebuttal?" (35:26), perhaps the reason for it is that their affair is solely with Allah; no one intervenes between Him and them to intercede for them or help them so they might escape the punishment.

His saying: وَمِنَ الْجِبَالِ جُدَدٌ بِيضٌ وَحُمْرٌ مُخْتَلِفٌ أَلْوَانُهَا وَغَرَابِيبُ سُودٌ — and in the mountains are stripes white and red, of varying colours, and others of intense black (35:27). The word judad (الجدد) is the plural of juddah (جدة), meaning a path or a track (الجادة). Bīḍ (البيض) and ḥumr (الحمر) are the plurals of white (أبيض) and red (أحمر). It appears that His saying: مُخْتَلِفٌ أَلْوَانُهَا — "of varying colours" is an adjective for judad, and "their colours" (أَلْوَانُهَا) is the subject (فاعل) of the participle "varying" (مُخْتَلِفٌ). If the phrase were a subject and predicate (مبتدأ وخبر), it would have been said mukhtalifatun alwānuhā (مختلفة ألوانها), as has been suggested. Gharābīb (الغرابيب) is the plural of ghirbīb, which is that which is intensely black (الشديد السواد), from which the word for crow (الغراب) is derived. Sūd (سُودٌ) is an appositive (بدل) or an explanatory connective (عطف بيان) for gharābīb.

The meaning is: Have you not seen that among the mountains are tracks that are white, red, and black, of varying colors? This refers either to the paths traveled in the mountains which have different colors, or to the mountains themselves, which are various lines extended across the face of the earth—white, red, and black, of varying colors.

His saying, the Exalted: وَمِنَ النَّاسِ وَالدَّوَابِّ وَالْأَنْعَامِ مُخْتَلِفٌ أَلْوَانُهُ كَذَلِكَ — And of humans and beasts and cattle there are likewise different colours (35:28). That is, among humans, the beasts (الدواب) that crawl upon the earth, and the cattle (الأنعام) such as camels, sheep, and cows, there are those whose colors vary in whiteness, redness, and blackness, just like the variation of fruits and mountains in their colors.

It has been said that His saying: كَذَلِكَ — "likewise" is the predicate (خبر) of a deleted subject (مبتدأ), the estimation being: "The matter is likewise." Thus, it is a summary affirmation of the preceding detail regarding the variation of fruits, mountains, humans, beasts, and cattle.

It has also been said that كَذَلِكَ is connected to His saying: يَخْشَى — "fears" in the verse: إِنَّمَا يَخْشَى اللهَ مِنْ عِبَادِهِ الْعُلَمَاءُ — "Only those of Allah’s servants having knowledge fear Him" (35:28). The reference would be to the aforementioned reflection upon the fruits, mountains, and others. The meaning would then be: "Only those of His servants who have knowledge fear Allah with such reflection upon the signs." However, this is far-fetched both linguistically and semantically.

His saying, the Exalted: إِنَّمَا يَخْشَى اللهَ مِنْ عِبَادِهِ الْعُلَمَاءُ — Only those of Allah’s servants having knowledge fear Him (35:28). This is an introductory sentence (استئناف) clarifying that reflecting upon these signs only produces its effect and bequeaths true faith in Allah and fear (الخشية) of Him in the full sense of the word in the scholars (العلماء), not the ignorant. It has already been mentioned that warning only succeeds with them, where He said: إِنَّمَا تُنْذِرُ الَّذِينَ يَخْشَوْنَ رَبَّهُمْ بِالْغَيْبِ وَأَقَامُوا الصَّلَاةَ — "You can only warn those who fear their Lord in secret and maintain the prayer" (35:18). Thus, this verse is like a clarification of the meaning of that one, explaining that fear—true fear—is only found in the scholars.

What is meant by "the scholars" (العلماء) are those who have knowledge of Allah; they are those who know Allah, the Glorified, through His names, attributes, and acts with a complete knowledge (معرفة) by which their hearts find tranquility, which removes the blemish of doubt and anxiety from their souls, and whose effects appear in their deeds, such that their actions confirm their words. What is meant by fear (الخشية) then is true fear, which is followed by inner humility (خشوع) and outward submission (خضوع). This is what the context demands for the meaning of the verse.

His saying: إِنَّ اللهَ عَزِيزٌ غَفُورٌ — Indeed Allah is all-mighty, all-forgiving (35:28) provides the sense of a justification (تعليل). Due to His might (عزة) and His being the Subduer (القاهر) who is never subdued, and the Victorious who is never defeated from any side, those who know Him fear Him. And because He is All-Forgiving (غفور), granting much forgiveness for sins and errors, they believe in Him, seek nearness to Him, and long for His meeting.

His saying, the Exalted: إِنَّ الَّذِينَ يَتْلُونَ كِتَابَ اللهِ وَأَقَامُوا الصَّلَاةَ وَأَنْفَقُوا مِمَّا رَزَقْنَاهُمْ سِرًّا وَعَلَانِيَةً يَرْجُونَ تِجَارَةً لَنْ تَبُورَ — Indeed those who recite the Book of Allah and maintain the prayer, and spend secretly and openly out of what We have provided them, expect a commerce that will never perish (35:29). The recitation (تلاوة) of the Book is the reading of the Quran, which Allah, the Glorified, has praised. Maintaining the prayer (إقامة الصلاة) is the persistence in performing it and guarding it against being abandoned. Spending from the provision secretly and openly (الإنفاق من الرزق سراً وعلانية) is the giving of wealth in secret to avoid ostentation (الرياء) and the loss of sincerity (الإخلاص) in recommended spending, and the giving of wealth openly so that it may spread among the people, as in obligatory spending.

His saying: يَرْجُونَ تِجَارَةً لَنْ تَبُورَ — "expect a commerce that will never perish" means it will never be destroyed by loss. Some have mentioned that His saying: يَرْجُونَ — "expect" etc., is the predicate (خبر) of inna (إِنَّ) at the beginning of the verse. According to others, the predicate is elided and connected to His saying: لِيُوَفِّيَهُمْ — "that He may pay them" etc., meaning: "They did what they did so that He may pay them their rewards in full."

His saying, the Exalted: لِيُوَفِّيَهُمْ أُجُورَهُمْ وَيَزِيدَهُمْ مِنْ فَضْلِهِ إِنَّهُ غَفُورٌ شَكُورٌ — that He may pay them their rewards in full and enhance them out of His grace. Indeed He is all-forgiving, all-appreciative (35:30). This is connected to His saying: يَتْلُونَ — "recite" and what is coordinated with it in the previous verse. That is, they performed what they performed so that He may pay them and grant them their rewards and the recompense for their deeds in a complete and perfect manner.

His saying: وَيَزِيدَهُمْ مِنْ فَضْلِهِ — "and enhance them out of His grace." It is possible that what is meant by this increase is the doubling of the reward many times over, as in His saying: مَنْ جَاءَ بِالْحَسَنَةِ فَلَهُ عَشْرُ أَمْثَالِهَا — "Whoever brings a good deed shall receive ten times its like" (6:160), and His saying: مَثَلُ الَّذِينَ يُنْفِقُونَ أَمْوَالَهُمْ فِي سَبِيلِ اللهِ كَمَثَلِ حَبَّةٍ أَنْبَتَتْ سَبْعَ سَنَابِلَ فِي كُلِّ سُنْبُلَةٍ مِائَةُ حَبَّةٍ وَاللهُ يُضَاعِفُ لِمَنْ يَشَاءُ — "The parable of those who spend their wealth in the way of Allah is that of a grain which grows seven ears, in every ear a hundred grains. Allah enhances severalfold whomever He wishes" (2:261). It is also possible that what is meant is an increase that is not of the same kind as the reward for deeds, as in His saying: لَهُمْ مَا يَشَاءُونَ فِيهَا وَلَدَيْنَا مَزِيدٌ — "There they will have whatever they wish, and with Us there is yet more" (50:35).

His saying: إِنَّهُ غَفُورٌ شَكُورٌ — "Indeed He is all-forgiving, all-appreciative" is a justification for the content of the verse and the increase. For He, the Exalted, because He is All-Forgiving (غفور), forgives their lapses, and because He is All-Appreciative (شكور), He rewards them and increases them from His grace.

His saying, the Exalted: وَالَّذِي أَوْحَيْنَا إِلَيْكَ مِنَ الْكِتَابِ هُوَ الْحَقُّ — That which We have revealed to you of the Book is the truth (35:31). The separating pronoun (ضمير الفصل) and the definite article in His saying: هُوَ الْحَقُّ — "is the truth" are for emphasis, not for restriction; meaning it is truth unmixed with falsehood.

His saying, the Exalted: ثُمَّ أَوْرَثْنَا الْكِتَابَ الَّذِينَ اصْطَفَيْنَا مِنْ عِبَادِنَا — Then We caused those whom We chose of Our servants to inherit the Book (35:32) until the end of the verse.

It is said: "He made him inherit (أورثه) such-and-such wealth," meaning he left it among them to manage after him, whereas he had been the one managing it and disposing of it. Likewise, the inheritance of knowledge, status, and the like is leaving it with another to manage after it was with someone else who benefited from it. Thus, making the people inherit the Book is leaving it with them so they may receive it generation after generation and benefit from it.

This attribution is correct even if those who manage it are some of the people rather than all of them. The Exalted said: وَلَقَدْ آتَيْنَا مُوسَى الْهُدَى وَأَوْرَثْنَا بَنِي إِسْرَائِيلَ الْكِتَابَ هُدًى وَذِكْرَى لِأُولِي الْأَلْبَابِ — "Certainly We gave Moses the guidance and We made the Children of Israel inherit the Book, as a guidance and admonition for those who possess intellect" (40:53-54). And He said: إِنَّا أَنْزَلْنَا التَّوْرَاةَ فِيهَا هُدًى وَنُورٌ يَحْكُمُ بِهَا النَّبِيُّونَ الَّذِينَ أَسْلَمُوا لِلَّذِينَ هَادُوا وَالرَّبَّانِيُّونَ وَالْأَحْبَارُ بِمَا اسْتُحْفِظُوا مِنْ كِتَابِ اللهِ — "We sent down the Torah containing guidance and light. The prophets, who had submitted, judged by it for the Jews, and so did the man of God and the scribes, as they were entrusted to guard the Book of Allah" (5:44). And He said: وَإِنَّ الَّذِينَ أُورِثُوا الْكِتَابَ مِنْ بَعْدِهِمْ لَفِي شَكٍّ مِنْهُ مُرِيبٍ — "and those who were made to inherit the Book after them are in a disquieting doubt about it" (42:14). Thus, the Children of Israel were made to inherit the Book even though those who fulfilled its right and managed its affairs were some of them, not all.

What is meant by "the Book" (الكتاب) in the verse, according to what the context provides, is the Noble Quran. How could it be otherwise when His saying in the previous verse: وَالَّذِي أَوْحَيْنَا إِلَيْكَ مِنَ الْكِتَابِ — "That which We have revealed to you of the Book" is explicit in this? Thus, the definite article in "the Book" (الكتاب) is for specific reference (العهد), not for the genus (الجنس). Therefore, no weight is given to the view of those who say the article is for the genus and that what is meant by the Book is any divinely revealed Book sent to the prophets.

Selection (الاصطفاء) is taking the choice part (صفوة) of a thing. It is close in meaning to choosing (الاختيار), the difference being that choosing is taking a thing from among others because it is the best (خير) of them, while selection is taking it from among them because it is the purest (صفوة) and most sincere part of them.

His saying: مِنْ عِبَادِنَا — "of Our servants." It is possible that "of" (مِنْ) is for clarification (التبيين), for beginning (الابتداء), or for partitivity (التبعيض). The closest to the mind is that it is clarificatory, as the Exalted has said: وَسَلَامٌ عَلَى عِبَادِهِ الَّذِينَ اصْطَفَى — "and peace be to His servants whom He has chosen" (27:59).

They have differed regarding who these chosen ones among His servants are. It has been said: they are the prophets. It has been said: they are the Children of Israel who are included in His saying: إِنَّ اللهَ اصْطَفَى آدَمَ وَنُوحًا وَآلَ إِبْرَاهِيمَ وَآلَ عِمْرَانَ عَلَى الْعَالَمِينَ — "Indeed Allah chose Adam and Noah, and the progeny of Abraham and the progeny of Imran above all the worlds" (3:33). It has been said: they are the nation (أمة) of Muhammad (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم), for they inherited the Quran from their Prophet; to it they return and from it they benefit—their scholars without intermediary and others through them. It has been said: they are the scholars of the Muhammadan nation.

It has also been said—and this is what is narrated from the two Sadiqs (peace be upon them) in many widely transmitted (مستفيضة) narrations—that what is meant by them is the progeny (ذرية) of the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) from the children of Fatimah (peace be upon her). They are those included in the progeny of Abraham in His saying: إِنَّ اللهَ اصْطَفَى آدَمَ وَنُوحًا وَآلَ إِبْرَاهِيمَ — "Indeed Allah chose Adam and Noah, and the progeny of Abraham" (3:33). The Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) explicitly stated their knowledge of the Quran, the correctness of their insight regarding it, and their inseparable attachment to it by his saying in the mutawatir (متواتر) — a narration transmitted by so many chains that its authenticity is certain — and agreed-upon hadith: إِنِّي تَارِكٌ فِيكُمُ الثَّقَلَيْنِ كِتَابَ اللهِ وَعِتْرَتِي أَهْلَ بَيْتِي لَنْ يَفْتَرِقَا حَتَّى يَرِدَا عَلَيَّ الْحَوْضَ — "I am leaving among you the two weighty things: the Book of Allah and my progeny, the people of my house; they shall never separate until they return to me at the Pond." Based on this, the meaning is: after We revealed the Quran to you—then (ثُمَّ) being for rank-based sequence (التراخي الرتبي)—We made your progeny inherit it, and they are those whom We chose from Our servants when We chose the progeny of Abraham. The annexation of "servants" (العباد) to the "Nūn" of grandeur is for honor (التشريف).

His saying: فَمِنْهُمْ ظَالِمٌ لِنَفْسِهِ وَمِنْهُمْ مُقْتَصِدٌ وَمِنْهُمْ سَابِقٌ بِالْخَيْرَاتِ — So some of them are wrongdoers to themselves, and some of them are average, and some of them are foremost in good works (35:32). It is possible that the pronoun in "some of them" (فَمِنْهُمْ) refers back to "those whom We chose" (الَّذِينَ اصْطَفَيْنَا). In that case, the three groups—the one who wrongs himself, the average one, and the one foremost in good deeds—would be partners in the inheritance, even if the true inheritor who knows the Book and preserves it is the one foremost in good deeds.

It is also possible that it refers back to "Our servants" (عِبَادِنَا)—without the annexation implying honor—so His saying "some of them" (فَمِنْهُمْ) would provide the sense of justification. The meaning would be: We only made some of Our servants inherit the Book, and they are the chosen ones, not all servants, because among Our servants are those who wrong themselves, those who are average, and those who are foremost, and not all are fit for inheritance.

The first of the two possibilities can be supported by the fact that there is no barrier to attributing inheritance to the whole while only some of them truly fulfill it, as we find its parallel in His saying, the Exalted: وَأَوْرَثْنَا بَنِي إِسْرَائِيلَ الْكِتَابَ — "and We made the Children of Israel inherit the Book" (40:54).

The contrast in the verse between the one who wrongs himself, the average one, and the one foremost in good deeds implies that what is meant by the "wrongdoer to himself" (الظالم لنفسه) is one who has some evil deeds upon him, yet he is a Muslim from the people of the Quran by virtue of being chosen and an inheritor. What is meant by the "average" (المقتصد) is the middle one who is on the right path (قصد السبيل) and the even way. What is meant by the "foremost in good works by Allah’s will" (سابق بالخيرات بإذن الله) is the one who has preceded the wrongdoer and the average one to the degrees of proximity (القرب); thus, he is an Imam for others by Allah's permission due to performing good deeds. The Exalted said: وَالسَّابِقُونَ السَّابِقُونَ أُولَئِكَ الْمُقَرَّبُونَ — "And the Foremost Ones are the foremost ones: they are the ones brought near [to Allah]" (56:10-11).

His saying, the Exalted: ذَلِكَ هُوَ الْفَضْلُ الْكَبِيرُ — That is the great grace (35:32). That is, the aforementioned inheritance is the great grace from Allah; personal acquisition (الكسب) has no part in it.

This is what the context provides and what the reports (الأخبار) indicate regarding the meaning of the verse. Within it, there is a strange disagreement among the commentators. They differed regarding "then" (ثُمَّ): it was said it is for sequence (التراخي) according to the reports; it was said it is for rank-based sequence (التراخي الرتبي); and it was said it is for temporal sequence (التراخي الزماني). Then, is the coordination (العطف) with "We revealed" (أَوْحَيْنَا) or with "that which We revealed" (الَّذِي أَوْحَيْنَا)?

They differed regarding "We caused to inherit" (أَوْرَثْنَا): it was said it is according to its literal meaning; it was said its meaning is "We decreed its inheritance and ordained it." They differed regarding "the Book" (الْكِتَابَ): it was said what is meant is the Quran; it was said the genus of divinely revealed books. They differed regarding "those whom We chose" (الَّذِينَ اصْطَفَيْنَا): it was said what is meant is the prophets; it was said the Children of Israel; it was said the nation of Muhammad; it was said the scholars among them; it was said the progeny of the Prophet from the children of Fatimah (peace be upon her).

They differed regarding "of Our servants" (مِنْ عِبَادِنَا): it was said "of" (مِنْ) is for partitivity, for beginning, or for clarification, and the meaning of "servants" differs according to the meaning of "of." Likewise, the annexation of "Our servants" is for honor according to some views and for other than that according to others.

They differed regarding "some of them" (فَمِنْهُمْ): it was said the referent of the pronoun is "those" (الَّذِينَ); it was said "Our servants" (عِبَادِنَا). They differed regarding the wrongdoer to himself, the average, and the foremost: it was said the wrongdoer is he whose outward is better than his inward, the average is he whose outward and inward are equal, and the foremost is he whose inward is better than his outward. It was said: the foremost are the foremost ones who passed away during the time of the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) from among his companions, the average are those who followed their tracks and joined them from the companions, and the wrongdoer to himself is anyone else. It was said: the wrongdoer is he whose evil deeds predominate, the average is of a middle state, and the foremost is the one brought near to Allah, foremost in degrees.

There are other scattered opinions that we have omitted; if one were to multiply the possibilities by each other, they would exceed a thousand.

His saying, the Exalted: جَنَّاتُ عَدْنٍ يَدْخُلُونَهَا يُحَلَّوْنَ فِيهَا مِنْ أَسَاوِرَ مِنْ ذَهَبٍ وَلُؤْلُؤًا وَلِبَاسُهُمْ فِيهَا حَرِيرٌ — [They will enter] the Gardens of Eden, where they will be adorned with bracelets of gold and pearls, and their dress there will be silk (35:33). Adornment (التحلية) is decoration. Asāwir (الأساور) is the plural of aswirah, which is the plural of siwār (bracelet). Al-Raghib said: the siwār of a woman is an Arabized word, and its origin is dastwārah. End quote.

His saying: جَنَّاتُ عَدْنٍ — "Gardens of Eden" etc. Its literal meaning is that it is an explanation of the "great grace." It is said in al-Majma‘: "This is an interpretation of the grace, as if it were said: 'What is that grace?' and He replied: 'It is Gardens,' meaning the reward of Gardens or the entry into Gardens. It is also permissible for it to be an appositive (بدل) for the grace, as if He said: 'That is the entry into Gardens.'" End quote. The rest is clear.

His saying, the Exalted: وَقَالُوا الْحَمْدُ لِلَّهِ الَّذِي أَذْهَبَ عَنَّا الْحَزَنَ إِنَّ رَبَّنَا لَغَفُورٌ شَكُورٌ — They will say, ‘All praise belongs to Allah, who has removed all grief from us. Indeed our Lord is all-forgiving, all-appreciative (35:34). It is said: what is meant by the grief (الحزن) for the removal of which they praise Allah upon their entry into Paradise is the grief that used to face them in the life of this world and the hardships and calamities that surround it.

It is also said: what is meant is the grief that had surrounded them after departing from the world and before entering the Paradise of the Hereafter, out of apprehension (إشفاق) regarding the evil deeds they had earned.

Based on this, the statement would be the saying of the wrongdoer to himself among them, or his saying and the saying of the average one. As for the one foremost in good deeds among them, there is no evil deed in the record of his deeds for which he would be punished. This view is more suitable for their saying at the end of their praise: إِنَّ رَبَّنَا لَغَفُورٌ شَكُورٌ — "Indeed our Lord is all-forgiving, all-appreciative."

His saying, the Exalted: الَّذِي أَحَلَّنَا دَارَ الْمُقَامَةِ مِنْ فَضْلِهِ لَا يَمَسُّنَا فِيهَا نَصَبٌ وَلَا يَمَسُّنَا فِيهَا لُغُوبٌ — who has settled us in the everlasting abode by His grace. In it neither weariness touches us, nor does fatigue touch us’ (35:35). Al-muqāmah (المقامة) is residence (الإقامة), and the "abode of residence" (دار المقامة) is the dwelling from which there is no exit and no departure.

Naṣab (النصب) is tiredness and hardship. Lughūb (اللغوب) is exhaustion and tiredness in seeking a livelihood and other things.

The meaning is: He who has made us residents in the abode of eternity by His grace, without any entitlement from us upon Him; no hardship or tiredness touches us in this abode—which is Paradise—nor does any exhaustion or weariness touch us in seeking what we desire; that is, we have therein whatever we wish.

In His saying: مِنْ فَضْلِهِ — "by His grace," there is a special correspondence with His previous saying: ذَلِكَ هُوَ الْفَضْلُ الْكَبِيرُ — "That is the great grace."

His saying, the Exalted: وَالَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا لَهُمْ نَارُ جَهَنَّمَ — As for the faithless, for them is the fire of hell (35:36) until the end of the verse. The "Lām" in "for them" (لَهُمْ) is for exclusivity (الاختصاص) and implies that the Fire is a recompense for them that shall not be separated from them. His saying: لَا يُقْضَى عَلَيْهِمْ فَيَمُوتُوا — "they will neither be done away with so that they might die" means no judgment of death will be passed upon them such that they die; thus, they are alive in the midst of the severity of the punishment they are in. وَلَا يُخَفَّفُ عَنْهُمْ مِنْ عَذَابِهَا كَذَلِكَ نَجْزِي كُلَّ كَفُورٍ — "nor will its punishment be lightened for them. Thus do We requite every ingrate" (35:36) who is intense in disbelief or frequent in it.

His saying, the Exalted: وَهُمْ يَصْطَرِخُونَ فِيهَا رَبَّنَا أَخْرِجْنَا — They will cry out in it, ‘Our Lord! Bring us out (35:37) until the end of the verse. In al-Majma‘: al-iṣṭirākh (الاصطراخ) is shouting and calling out for help; it is the ifti‘āl form of ṣurākh (screaming). End quote.

His saying: رَبَّنَا أَخْرِجْنَا — "Our Lord! Bring us out" etc., is an explanation of their crying out. His saying: أَوَلَمْ نُعَمِّرْكُمْ مَا يَتَذَكَّرُ فِيهِ مَنْ تَذَكَّرَ — "Did We not let you live long enough for him who could take admonition to take it?" (35:37) etc., is the response to their crying out. His saying: فَذُوقُوا — "So taste!" and His saying: فَمَا لِلظَّالِمِينَ مِنْ نَصِيرٍ — "for the wrongdoers there is no helper" (35:37) are each branched from what precedes them.

The meaning is: these disbelievers who are in the Fire cry out and shout for help therein, saying: "Our Lord! Bring us out of the Fire so we may do righteous deeds, not evil ones, other than what we used to do." It is said to them in rebuttal: "Nay! Did We not grant you a lifespan in which he who would take admonition could take it, and the warner came to you and warned you of this punishment, yet you did not take admonition and did not believe? So taste the punishment, for the wrongdoers have no helper to help them so they might be delivered from the punishment."

His saying, the Exalted: إِنَّ اللهَ عَالِمُ غَيْبِ السَّمَاوَاتِ وَالْأَرْضِ إِنَّهُ عَلِيمٌ بِذَاتِ الصُّدُورِ — Indeed Allah is the Knower of the Unseen of the heavens and the earth. Indeed He knows best what is in the breasts (35:38). Thus, He deals with you according to what is in your interior of belief and the effects of deeds, and He holds you accountable for it, whether your outward matches your inward or differs from it. The Exalted said: إِنْ تُبْدُوا مَا فِي أَنْفُسِكُمْ أَوْ تُخْفُوهُ يُحَاسِبْكُمْ بِهِ اللهُ — "Whether you reveal what is in your hearts or hide it, Allah will bring you to account for it" (2:284), and He said: يَوْمَ تُبْلَى السَّرَائِرُ — "on the day when the secrets are examined" (86:9).

(Narrative Discussion)

In al-Majma‘, regarding His saying, the Exalted: إِنَّمَا يَخْشَى اللهَ مِنْ عِبَادِهِ الْعُلَمَاءُ — "Only those of Allah’s servants having knowledge fear Him" (35:28): It is narrated from Imam al-Sadiq (peace be upon him) that he said: يَعْنِي بِالْعُلَمَاءِ مَنْ صَدَّقَ قَوْلَهُ فِعْلُهُ، وَمَنْ لَمْ يُصَدِّقْ فِعْلُهُ قَوْلَهُ فَلَيْسَ بِعَالِمٍ — "He means by 'the scholars' those whose actions confirm their words; and whoever's actions do not confirm his words is not a scholar." And in the hadith: أَعْلَمُكُمْ بِاللهِ أَخْوَفُكُمْ لِلَّهِ — "The most knowledgeable of you regarding Allah is the one who fears Allah the most."

I say: In Rawḍat al-Kāfī, with its chain of transmission from Abu Hamzah from Imam Ali ibn al-Husayn (peace be upon him), there is something of the same meaning.

In al-Durr al-Manthūr, Ibn Abi Shaybah, al-Tirmidhi, and al-Hakim narrated from al-Hasan that he said: The Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) said: الْعِلْمُ عِلْمَانِ: عِلْمٌ فِي الْقَلْبِ فَذَاكَ الْعِلْمُ النَّافِعُ، وَعِلْمٌ عَلَى اللِّسَانِ فَذَاكَ حُجَّةُ اللهِ عَلَى خَلْقِهِ — "Knowledge is of two types: knowledge in the heart, and that is the beneficial knowledge; and knowledge on the tongue, and that is Allah's proof against His creation."

In al-Majma‘, Ibn Mas‘ud narrated from the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) that he said regarding His saying: وَيَزِيدَهُمْ مِنْ فَضْلِهِ — "and enhance them out of His grace": هُوَ الشَّفَاعَةُ لِمَنْ وَجَبَتْ لَهُ النَّارُ ـ مِمَّنْ صَنَعَ إِلَيْهِ مَعْرُوفاً فِي الدُّنْيَا — "It is the intercession (الشفاعة) for those for whom the Fire has become mandatory—from among those who did a favor for him in the world."

In al-Kāfī, with its chain of transmission from Ahmad ibn ‘Umar, he said: I asked Abu al-Hasan al-Rida (peace be upon him) about the saying of Allah, the Mighty and Majestic: ثُمَّ أَوْرَثْنَا الْكِتَابَ الَّذِينَ اصْطَفَيْنَا مِنْ عِبَادِنَا — "Then We caused those whom We chose of Our servants to inherit the Book" (35:32). He said: وَلَدُ فَاطِمَةَ عَلَيْهَا السَّلَامُ، وَالسَّابِقُ بِالْخَيْرَاتِ الْإِمَامُ ـ وَالْمُقْتَصِدُ الْعَارِفُ بِالْإِمَامِ ـ وَالظَّالِمُ لِنَفْسِهِ الَّذِي لَا يَعْرِفُ الْإِمَامَ — "The children of Fatimah (peace be upon her). The one foremost in good deeds is the Imam; the average one is the one who knows the Imam; and the one who wrongs himself is the one who does not know the Imam."

From the book Sa‘d al-Su‘ūd by Ibn Tawus, in a hadith from Abu Ishaq al-Sabi‘i from Imam al-Baqir (peace be upon him) regarding the verse, he said: هِيَ لَنَا خَاصَّةً يَا أَبَا إِسْحَاقَ ـ أَمَّا السَّابِقُ بِالْخَيْرَاتِ فَعَلِيُّ بْنُ أَبِي طَالِبٍ ـ وَالْحَسَنُ وَالْحُسَيْنُ وَالشَّهِيدُ مِنَّا، وَأَمَّا الْمُقْتَصِدُ فَصَائِمٌ بِالنَّهَارِ وَقَائِمٌ بِاللَّيْلِ، وَأَمَّا الظَّالِمُ لِنَفْسِهِ فَفِيهِ مَا فِي النَّاسِ وَهُوَ مَغْفُورٌ لَهُ — "It is for us specifically, O Abu Ishaq. As for the one foremost in good deeds, it is Ali ibn Abi Talib, al-Hasan, al-Husayn, and the Witness (الشهيد) from among us. As for the average one, he is one who fasts by day and stands [in prayer] by night. As for the one who wrongs himself, he has within him what is in the people, and he is forgiven."

I say: What is meant by "the Witness" (الشهيد), by the evidence of other narrations, is the Imam.

In Ma‘ānī al-Akhbār, narrated from Imam al-Sadiq (peace be upon him) regarding the verse, he said: الظَّالِمُ يَحُومُ حَوْمَ نَفْسِهِ ـ وَالْمُقْتَصِدُ يَحُومُ حَوْمَ قَلْبِهِ ـ وَالسَّابِقُ بِالْخَيْرَاتِ يَحُومُ حَوْمَ رَبِّهِ — "The wrongdoer hovers around his self; the average one hovers around his heart; and the one foremost in good deeds hovers around his Lord."

I say: al-ḥawm and al-ḥawamān (الحوم والحومان) mean circling. The circling of the one who wrongs himself around his self is his following its desires and his striving to attain what pleases it. The circling of the average one around his heart is his preoccupation with what purifies his heart and cleanses it through asceticism (الزهد) and worship (التعبد). The circling of the one foremost in good deeds around his Lord is his sincerity (إخلاص) to Him, the Exalted, such that he remembers Him and forgets everything else, hoping for none but Him and intending none but Him.

Know that the narrations from the paths of the Shi‘a from the Imams of the People of the House (peace be upon them) regarding the verse being specific to the children of Fatimah (peace be upon her) are very numerous.

In al-Durr al-Manthūr, al-Faryabi, Ahmad, ‘Abd ibn Humayd, Ibn Jarir, Ibn al-Mundhir, Ibn Abi Hatim, al-Tabarani, al-Hakim, Ibn Mardawayh, and al-Bayhaqi narrated from Abu al-Darda: I heard the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) saying: Allah, the Exalted, said: ثُمَّ أَوْرَثْنَا الْكِتَابَ الَّذِينَ اصْطَفَيْنَا مِنْ عِبَادِنَا ـ فَمِنْهُمْ ظَالِمٌ لِنَفْسِهِ وَمِنْهُمْ مُقْتَصِدٌ ـ وَمِنْهُمْ سَابِقٌ بِالْخَيْرَاتِ بِإِذْنِ اللهِ — "Then We caused those whom We chose of Our servants to inherit the Book: so some of them are wrongdoers to themselves, and some of them are average, and some of them are foremost in good works by Allah’s will" (35:32). As for those who were foremost, they shall enter Paradise without reckoning. As for those who were average, they are those who will be reckoned with an easy reckoning. As for those who wronged themselves, they are those who will be detained throughout the gathering (المحشر), then they are those whom Allah will meet with mercy; they are the ones who say: الْحَمْدُ لِلَّهِ الَّذِي أَذْهَبَ عَنَّا الْحَزَنَ ـ إِنَّ رَبَّنَا لَغَفُورٌ شَكُورٌ ـ الَّذِي أَحَلَّنَا دَارَ الْمُقَامَةِ مِنْ فَضْلِهِ ـ لَا يَمَسُّنَا فِيهَا نَصَبٌ وَلَا يَمَسُّنَا فِيهَا لُغُوبٌ — "All praise belongs to Allah, who has removed all grief from us. Indeed our Lord is all-forgiving, all-appreciative, who has settled us in the everlasting abode by His grace. In it neither weariness touches us, nor does fatigue touch us" (35:34-35).

I say: It was narrated in al-Majma‘ from Abu al-Darda from him (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم). There are other hadiths with the same meaning, and there are those that contradict them which are not given weight, such as what is in it from Ibn Mardawayh from ‘Umar from the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) regarding His saying: فَمِنْهُمْ ظَالِمٌ لِنَفْسِهِ — "so some of them are wrongdoers to themselves": he said, "The disbeliever."

In the Tafsir of al-Qummi, regarding His saying, the Exalted: لَا يَمَسُّنَا ـ فِيهَا نَصَبٌ وَلَا يَمَسُّنَا فِيهَا لُغُوبٌ — "In it neither weariness touches us, nor does fatigue touch us": he said, al-naṣab (النصب) is hardship (العناء) and al-lughūb (اللغوب) is laziness and annoyance (الضجر).

In Nahj al-Balāghah, he (peace be upon him) said: الْعُمُرُ الَّذِي أَعْذَرَ اللهُ فِيهِ إِلَى ابْنِ آدَمَ سِتُّونَ سَنَةً — "The lifespan for which Allah has left no excuse for the son of Adam is sixty years."

I say: It was narrated from him (peace be upon him) in al-Majma‘, and it was narrated in al-Durr al-Manthūr from Ibn Jarir from him (peace be upon him).

In al-Durr al-Manthūr, al-Hakim al-Tirmidhi in Nawādir al-Uṣūl, al-Bayhaqi in his Sunan, Ibn Jarir, Ibn al-Mundhir, Ibn Abi Hatim, al-Tabarani, Ibn Mardawayh, and al-Bayhaqi in Shu‘ab al-Īmān narrated from Ibn ‘Abbas that the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) said: إِذَا كَانَ يَوْمُ الْقِيَامَةِ قِيلَ: أَيْنَ أَبْنَاءُ السِّتِّينَ وَهُوَ الْمُعَمَّرُ الَّذِي قَالَ اللهُ: أَوَلَمْ نُعَمِّرْكُمْ مَا يَتَذَكَّرُ فِيهِ مَنْ تَذَكَّرَ — "When the Day of Resurrection comes, it will be said: 'Where are the sixty-year-olds?' and that is the long-lived one (المعمر) of whom Allah said: 'Did We not let you live long enough for him who could take admonition to take it?'"

I say: This has been narrated through other paths from Sahl ibn Sa‘d and Abu Hurayrah from him (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم).

In al-Majma‘: It is said it is a rebuke for the eighteen-year-old; and this was narrated from Imam al-Baqir (peace be upon him).

I say: It was narrated in al-Faqīh from him (peace be upon him) as a muḍmar (مضمر) — a narration where the name of the Imam is replaced by a pronoun.

Ayahs

هُوَ ٱلَّذِى جَعَلَكُمْ خَلَـٰٓئِفَ فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ ۚ فَمَن كَفَرَ فَعَلَيْهِ كُفْرُهُۥ ۖ وَلَا يَزِيدُ ٱلْكَـٰفِرِينَ كُفْرُهُمْ عِندَ رَبِّهِمْ إِلَّا مَقْتًا ۖ وَلَا يَزِيدُ ٱلْكَـٰفِرِينَ كُفْرُهُمْ إِلَّا خَسَارًا (٣٩)
It is He who made you successors on the earth. So whoever is faithless, his unfaith is to his own detriment. The unfaith of the faithless does not increase them with their Lord [in anything] except disfavour, and their unfaith increases the faithless in nothing except loss. (39)
قُلْ أَرَءَيْتُمْ شُرَكَآءَكُمُ ٱلَّذِينَ تَدْعُونَ مِن دُونِ ٱللَّهِ أَرُونِى مَاذَا خَلَقُوا۟ مِنَ ٱلْأَرْضِ أَمْ لَهُمْ شِرْكٌ فِى ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ أَمْ ءَاتَيْنَـٰهُمْ كِتَـٰبًا فَهُمْ عَلَىٰ بَيِّنَتٍ مِّنْهُ ۚ بَلْ إِن يَعِدُ ٱلظَّـٰلِمُونَ بَعْضُهُم بَعْضًا إِلَّا غُرُورًا (٤٠)
Say, ‘Tell me about your ‘partners’ whom you invoke besides Allah? Show me what [part] of the earth have they created. Have they any share in the heavens?’ Have We given them a scripture so that they stand on a manifest proof from it? No, the wrongdoers do not promise one another [anything] except delusion. (40)
۞ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ يُمْسِكُ ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَٱلْأَرْضَ أَن تَزُولَا ۚ وَلَئِن زَالَتَآ إِنْ أَمْسَكَهُمَا مِنْ أَحَدٍ مِّنۢ بَعْدِهِۦٓ ۚ إِنَّهُۥ كَانَ حَلِيمًا غَفُورًا (٤١)
Indeed Allah sustains the heavens and the earth lest they should fall apart, and if they were to fall apart, there is none who can sustain them except Him. Indeed He is all-forbearing, all-forgiving. (41)
وَأَقْسَمُوا۟ بِٱللَّهِ جَهْدَ أَيْمَـٰنِهِمْ لَئِن جَآءَهُمْ نَذِيرٌ لَّيَكُونُنَّ أَهْدَىٰ مِنْ إِحْدَى ٱلْأُمَمِ ۖ فَلَمَّا جَآءَهُمْ نَذِيرٌ مَّا زَادَهُمْ إِلَّا نُفُورًا (٤٢)
They had sworn by Allah with solemn oaths that if a warner were to come to them, they would be better guided than any of the nations. But when a warner came to them, it only increased their distance [from the truth], (42)
ٱسْتِكْبَارًا فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ وَمَكْرَ ٱلسَّيِّئِ ۚ وَلَا يَحِيقُ ٱلْمَكْرُ ٱلسَّيِّئُ إِلَّا بِأَهْلِهِۦ ۚ فَهَلْ يَنظُرُونَ إِلَّا سُنَّتَ ٱلْأَوَّلِينَ ۚ فَلَن تَجِدَ لِسُنَّتِ ٱللَّهِ تَبْدِيلًا ۖ وَلَن تَجِدَ لِسُنَّتِ ٱللَّهِ تَحْوِيلًا (٤٣)
due to their domineering [conduct] in the land and their devising of evil schemes; and evil schemes beset only their authors. So do they await anything except the precedent of the ancients? Yet you will never find any change in Allah’s precedent, and you will never find any revision in Allah’s precedent. (43)
أَوَلَمْ يَسِيرُوا۟ فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ فَيَنظُرُوا۟ كَيْفَ كَانَ عَـٰقِبَةُ ٱلَّذِينَ مِن قَبْلِهِمْ وَكَانُوٓا۟ أَشَدَّ مِنْهُمْ قُوَّةً ۚ وَمَا كَانَ ٱللَّهُ لِيُعْجِزَهُۥ مِن شَىْءٍ فِى ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَلَا فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ ۚ إِنَّهُۥ كَانَ عَلِيمًا قَدِيرًا (٤٤)
Have they not travelled over the land so that they may observe how was the fate of those who were before them? They were more powerful than them, and Allah is not to be frustrated by anything in the heavens or on the earth. Indeed, He is all-knowing, all-powerful. (44)
وَلَوْ يُؤَاخِذُ ٱللَّهُ ٱلنَّاسَ بِمَا كَسَبُوا۟ مَا تَرَكَ عَلَىٰ ظَهْرِهَا مِن دَآبَّةٍ وَلَـٰكِن يُؤَخِّرُهُمْ إِلَىٰٓ أَجَلٍ مُّسَمًّى ۖ فَإِذَا جَآءَ أَجَلُهُمْ فَإِنَّ ٱللَّهَ كَانَ بِعِبَادِهِۦ بَصِيرًۢا (٤٥)
Were Allah to take humans to task because of what they have earned, He would not leave any living being on its back. But He respites them until a specified time, and when their time comes, [He retributes them in accordance with their works], for Allah has been watching His servants. (45)

(Commentary)

This is an argumentation for the monotheism of Lordship (توحيد الربوبية) — the absolute oneness of Allah in His governance and management of the universe — similar to His saying: هُوَ الَّذِي جَعَلَكُمْ خَلائِفَ فِي الْأَرْضِ "It is He who made you successors on the earth" (35:39), and His saying: إِنَّ اللهَ يُمْسِكُ السَّماواتِ وَالْأَرْضَ أَنْ تَزُولا "Indeed Allah sustains the heavens and the earth lest they should decline" (35:41). It is also an argumentation for the negation of the lordship of their partners: قُلْ أَرَأَيْتُمْ شُرَكاءَكُمُ الَّذِينَ تَدْعُونَ مِنْ دُونِ اللهِ "Say, ‘Tell me about your partners whom you invoke besides Allah’" (35:40). Furthermore, it serves as a rebuke and a threat to them for breaking what they had solemnized with oaths and for their evil plotting.

Then, it records that nothing can frustrate Allah, and that He only grants respite to those among these wrongdoers (الظالمين) whom He chooses until a specified term (أجل مسمى). When their term arrives, He will requite them with what they deserve. With this, the Surah concludes.

His saying, the Exalted: هُوَ الَّذِي جَعَلَكُمْ خَلائِفَ فِي الْأَرْضِ "It is He who made you successors on the earth..." Successors (الخلائف) is the plural of successor (خليفة). The fact that people are successors on the earth means that each subsequent generation takes the place of its predecessor, possessing the authority to manage and benefit from the earth just as the predecessor was empowered over it. They only attained this succession (الخلافة) by virtue of the nature of their creation—namely, creation through lineage and birth—for this mode of creation divides the created beings into predecessors (سلف) and successors (خلف).

Thus, the establishment of the earthly succession is a type of governance (تدبير) intertwined with creation (خلق) and inseparable from it. For this reason, it is used as evidence for His uniqueness in His Lordship (الربوبية), because it is exclusive to Allah, the Exalted; there is no room for anyone else to claim it.

Therefore, His saying: هُوَ الَّذِي جَعَلَكُمْ خَلائِفَ فِي الْأَرْضِ "It is He who made you successors on the earth" is a proof for His uniqueness in His Lordship.

As for its negation regarding their partners: the explanation is that the One who established earthly succession in the human world is their Lord, the Manager of their affairs. The establishment of succession is inseparable from the nature of creation; thus, the Creator of man is the Lord of man. Since the Creator is Allah, the Glorified—even according to the opponent—then Allah is the Lord of man.

His saying: فَمَنْ كَفَرَ فَعَلَيْهِ كُفْرُهُ "So whoever disbelieves, his disbelief will be to his own loss." That is, since Allah, the Glorified, is the Lord of man, then whoever disbelieves and conceals this reality, attributing Lordship to other than Allah, the Exalted, then the harm of his disbelief is upon himself.

His saying: وَلا يَزِيدُ الْكافِرِينَ كُفْرُهُمْ عِنْدَ رَبِّهِمْ إِلَّا مَقْتاً وَلا يَزِيدُ الْكافِرِينَ كُفْرُهُمْ إِلَّا خَساراً "and the disbelief of the faithless does not increase them with their Lord except in abhorrence, and the disbelief of the faithless does not increase them except in loss." This is an explanation of how their disbelief is against them: their disbelief earns them abhorrence (المقت) with their Lord—and abhorrence is the intensity of hatred—because it involves turning away from His servitude (العبودية) and showing disdain for His presence. It also earns them loss (خساراً) within themselves, because they have exchanged human felicity (السعادة) for wretchedness and calamity that will afflict them in their journey and their return to the Abode of Requital.

The effect of disbelief was expressed as an "increase" (الزيادة) because the human primordial nature (الفطرة) — the innate disposition toward truth that Allah placed in every human being — is simple and pristine, situated in a state of potential perfection and increase. If a person submits (أَسلم), it increases him in perfection and proximity to Allah; if he disbelieves, it increases him in abhorrence with Allah and in loss.

He restricted abhorrence with the phrase عِنْدَ رَبِّهِمْ "with their Lord," but did not do so for "loss," because loss is a consequence of exchanging faith (الإيمان) for disbelief and felicity for wretchedness, which is a matter pertaining to their own selves. As for abhorrence and intense hatred, it is from Allah, the Glorified.

Love (الحب) and hatred (البغض) attributed to Allah, the Glorified, are among the attributes of action (صفات الأفعال). These are meanings external to the Essence (الذات) and do not subsist within It. The meaning of His love for someone is the expansion of His mercy (الرحمة) over them and its attraction toward them, while His hatred for someone is the contraction of His mercy from them and its distance from them.

His saying, the Exalted: قُلْ أَرَأَيْتُمْ شُرَكاءَكُمُ الَّذِينَ تَدْعُونَ مِنْ دُونِ اللهِ "Say, ‘Tell me about your partners whom you invoke besides Allah’" to the end of the verse. The attribution of "partners" to them is based on the consideration that they claim they are partners to Allah; thus, it is a figurative attribution of possession.

In this verse, the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) is taught the argument for negating the lordship of their deities whom they used to worship. The formulation of the argument is that if they were lords and deities besides Allah, they would have some part in the governance of the world. Consequently, they would be creators of what they govern, because creation and governance are inseparable. If they were creators, there would be evidence for it. Evidence is either from the world or from Allah, the Glorified. As for the world, nothing in it indicates being created by them, even by way of partnership; this is His saying: أَرُونِي ما ذا خَلَقُوا مِنَ الْأَرْضِ أَمْ لَهُمْ شِرْكٌ فِي السَّماواتِ "show me what they have created of the earth. Do they have any share in the heavens?"

As for evidence from Him, the Exalted, if it existed, it would be a heavenly book revealed from Him, the Glorified, acknowledging their lordship and permitting people to worship them and take them as gods. Yet, no book has been revealed with this description, and they admit this; this is His saying: أَمْ آتَيْناهُمْ كِتاباً فَهُمْ عَلى بَيِّنَةٍ مِنْهُ "Have We given them a Book so that they stand on a clear proof from it?"

He expressed the negation of their creativity on earth with the phrase: أَرُونِي ما ذا خَلَقُوا مِنَ الْأَرْضِ "show me what they have created of the earth," and did not say: "Inform me, do they have a share in the earth?" However, regarding the heavens, He said: أَمْ لَهُمْ شِرْكٌ فِي السَّماواتِ "Do they have any share in the heavens?" and did not say: "Or what have they created of the heavens?"

This is because what is meant by "the earth"—as indicated by the context of the argumentation—is the terrestrial world, which is the earth and whatever is in it and upon it. What is meant by "the heavens" is the celestial world, comprising the heavens and whatever is in them and upon them. Thus, His saying: ما ذا خَلَقُوا مِنَ الْأَرْضِ "what they have created of the earth" carries the meaning of "do they have a share in the earth," which can only occur through the creation of something within it. His saying: أَمْ لَهُمْ شِرْكٌ فِي السَّماواتِ "Do they have any share in the heavens?" carries the meaning of "or what have they created of the heavens." He sufficed by mentioning creation on the side of the earth to indicate that partnership in Lordship can only exist through creation.

His saying: أَمْ آتَيْناهُمْ كِتاباً فَهُمْ عَلى بَيِّنَةٍ مِنْهُ "Have We given them a Book so that they stand on a clear proof from it?" means: "Rather, have We given them a book such that they possess a manifest proof (بينة) from that book that their partners have a share with Us?"—by its indicating that they are partners to Allah.

He said: أَمْ آتَيْناهُمْ كِتاباً "Have We given them a Book" and did not say: "Or do they have a book" or similar phrases, to emphasize the negation and denial. For saying "Do they have a book" is a denial of the book's existence, but His saying "Have We given them a Book" is a denial of the book's existence from the One who would reveal the book, were it to be revealed.

It has become clear from the preceding that the plural pronoun in آتَيْناهُمْ "We gave them" and in فَهُمْ عَلى بَيِّنَةٍ "so they [stand] on a clear proof" refers to the polytheists (المشركين). Therefore, no weight should be given to the opinion that these two pronouns refer to the "partners."

His saying: بَلْ إِنْ يَعِدُ الظَّالِمُونَ بَعْضُهُمْ بَعْضاً إِلَّا غُرُوراً "No, the wrongdoers do not promise one another anything but delusion." This is a transition (إضراب) from the previous argumentation, stating that what led them to polytheism (الشرك) was not a proof they relied upon, but rather the mutual delusion (غرور) of one another through promises of intercession (الشفاعة) — the act of mediating on behalf of others before Allah — and proximity. Their predecessors deluded their successors, and their leaders and chiefs deluded their subordinates and followers, promising them the intercession of the partners with Allah, the Glorified, while there is no reality to it.

The argument of the verse is general, applying to the polytheists who worship idols—those who worship angels, jinn, and saintly humans and take idols for them to turn toward; to those who worship the spiritual entities of the planets and turn toward the planets, then take idols for the planets; to those who worship angels and the elements without taking idols for them, as is reported of the ancient Persians; and to those who worship certain humans, such as the Christians' worship of the Messiah (عليه السلام).

His saying, the Exalted: إِنَّ اللهَ يُمْسِكُ السَّماواتِ وَالْأَرْضَ أَنْ تَزُولا وَلَئِنْ زالَتا إِنْ أَمْسَكَهُما مِنْ أَحَدٍ مِنْ بَعْدِهِ "Indeed Allah sustains the heavens and the earth lest they should decline, and were they to decline, there is none who can sustain them besides Him..." (35:41). It is said that the verse is a new sentence (استئناف) confirming the ultimate foulness and horror of polytheism. That is, Allah, the Exalted, preserves the heavens and the earth out of aversion to their declining, or so that they do not decline and vanish; for the contingent being (الممكن), just as it needs the Necessary Being (الواجب) at the moment of its origination, needs Him at the moment of its persistence. [End of quote].

What is apparent is that when He, the Exalted, argued for His uniqueness in Lordship by establishing succession in the human species with His saying: هُوَ الَّذِي جَعَلَكُمْ خَلائِفَ فِي الْأَرْضِ "It is He who made you successors on the earth," and then negated partnership absolutely through proof, He then generalized the proof to encompass the entire creation—namely, the heavens and the earth. He argued for His uniqueness by the preservation of creation after its origination. For it is manifest and beyond doubt that the origination (حدوث) of a thing and its initial donning of existence after non-existence is distinct from its persistence (بقاء) and its donning of existence after existence in a continuous manner. The persistence of a thing after its origination requires the bestowal of existence after existence in a connected and continuous fashion.

The preservation of a thing after its origination, just as it is a bestowal of existence after existence, is also a governance (تدبير) of its affairs. If you look closely, you will find that the order prevailing in the cosmos only proceeds through origination and preservation. The Originator and Creator is Allah, the Glorified—even according to the opponent—so Allah, the Glorified, is the sole Creator and Governor of the heavens and the earth, having no partner.

Thus, His saying: إِنَّ اللهَ يُمْسِكُ السَّماواتِ وَالْأَرْضَ أَنْ تَزُولا "Indeed Allah sustains the heavens and the earth lest they should decline." Sustaining (الإمساك) is used in its well-known sense, and His saying أَنْ تَزُولا "lest they should decline"—the implied meaning being "out of aversion to their declining" or "so that they do not decline"—is connected to it. It is also said that sustaining means prevention or preservation. In any case, sustaining is a metaphor for preservation (الإبقاء), which is the bestowal of existence after existence in a connected and continuous way. Declining (الزوال) is vanishing and nullification.

It is reported from some that they interpreted "declining" as spatial movement (الانتقال المكاني). The meaning would be that Allah prevents the heavens and the earth from any part of them moving from its established place, whether rising or falling. [End of quote]. The challenge lies in conceiving his intended meaning correctly.

His saying: وَلَئِنْ زالَتا إِنْ أَمْسَكَهُما مِنْ أَحَدٍ مِنْ بَعْدِهِ "and were they to decline, there is none who can sustain them besides Him." The context suggests that "declining" here means being on the verge of declining, for actual declining cannot coexist with sustaining. The meaning is: "I swear, if they were on the verge of declining, none could sustain them after Allah, the Glorified, for there is no bestower of existence (مفيض للوجود) other than Him." It is also possible that "declining" is meant in its literal sense, and "sustaining" refers to the power to sustain. It has been clarified that the first مِنْ is extra for emphasis, and the second is for beginning (للابتداء). The pronoun in مِنْ بَعْدِهِ "besides Him" refers to Him, the Exalted; it is also said it refers to the "declining."

His saying: إِنَّهُ كانَ حَلِيماً غَفُوراً "Indeed He is All-forbearing, All-forgiving." Due to His forbearance (حلم), He does not hasten any matter, and due to His forgiveness (مغفرة), He veils the aspects of non-existence in things. The requirement of these two Names is that He sustains the heavens and the earth lest they decline until a specified term.

It is said in Irshad al-Aql al-Salim: "إِنَّهُ كانَ حَلِيماً غَفُوراً 'Indeed He is All-forbearing, All-forgiving'—not hastening the punishment necessitated by their crimes, whereby He sustained them when they were worthy of being utterly demolished, as He, the Exalted, said: تَكادُ السَّماواتُ يَتَفَطَّرْنَ مِنْهُ وَتَنْشَقُّ الْأَرْضُ 'The heavens are about to be rent apart at it, and the earth to split asunder' (19:90)." [End of quote].

His saying, the Exalted: وَأَقْسَمُوا بِاللهِ جَهْدَ أَيْمانِهِمْ لَئِنْ جاءَهُمْ نَذِيرٌ لَيَكُونُنَّ أَهْدى مِنْ إِحْدَى الْأُمَمِ فَلَمَّا جاءَهُمْ نَذِيرٌ ما زادَهُمْ إِلَّا نُفُوراً "They swore by Allah with their most solemn oaths that if a warner should come to them, they would surely be better guided than any of the nations. But when a warner came to them, it only increased them in aversion" (35:42). Al-Raghib said: "الجهد (al-jahd)—with a fathah on the jim—and الجهد (al-juhd)—with a dammah—mean power and hardship..." until he said: "And He, the Exalted, said: وَأَقْسَمُوا بِاللهِ جَهْدَ أَيْمانِهِمْ 'They swore by Allah with their most solemn oaths,' meaning they swore and exerted themselves in the oath to deliver it in the most emphatic way within their power." [End of quote].

He also said: "النفر (al-nafr) is the agitation away from something or toward something, like fleeing toward or away from something. It is said: 'He recoiled (نفر) from the thing in aversion (نفورا).' He, the Exalted, said: ما زادَهُمْ إِلَّا نُفُوراً 'it only increased them in aversion.'" [End of quote].

It is said (1) that before the mission of the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم), word reached the Quraysh that the People of the Book had denied their messengers. They said: "May Allah curse the Jews and the Christians; messengers came to them and they denied them. By Allah, if a messenger comes to us, we shall surely be better guided than any of the nations." [End of quote]. The context of the verse confirms and supports this report.

Thus, His saying: وَأَقْسَمُوا بِاللهِ جَهْدَ أَيْمانِهِمْ "They swore by Allah with their most solemn oaths," the pronoun refers to the Quraysh. They swore this oath before the mission of the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم), as evidenced by His subsequent saying: فَلَمَّا جاءَهُمْ نَذِيرٌ "But when a warner came to them." The object of the oath is His saying: لَئِنْ جاءَهُمْ نَذِيرٌ "if a warner should come to them..."

His saying: لَئِنْ جاءَهُمْ نَذِيرٌ لَيَكُونُنَّ أَهْدى مِنْ إِحْدَى الْأُمَمِ "if a warner should come to them, they would surely be better guided than any of the nations"—meaning one of the nations to whom a warner had come, like the Jews and the Christians. He said: لَيَكُونُنَّ أَهْدى مِنْ إِحْدَى الْأُمَمِ "they would surely be better guided than any of the nations" and did not say: "better guided than them," because the meaning is that they were a nation to whom no warner had come; then, if a warner came to them, they would become a nation possessing a warner like one of those warned nations. Then, by believing the warner, they would become better guided than the one they resembled; this is His saying: أَهْدى مِنْ إِحْدَى الْأُمَمِ "better guided than any of the nations." So understand this.

It is also said that the context requires generality, and His saying إِحْدَى الْأُمَمِ "any of the nations" is general, even though it is an indefinite noun in an affirmative context. The definite article in الْأُمَمِ "the nations" is for reference (للعهد). The meaning is: "they would surely be better guided than every single one of those nations who denied their messengers, from the Jews, the Christians, and others."

It is also said the meaning is: "they would surely be better guided than a nation of whom it is said: 'one of the nations,' to distinguish it over other nations, just as it is said: 'he is the one of the people' or 'the one of his age.'" [End of quote]. This last view is not free from affectation and far-fetchedness.

His saying: فَلَمَّا جاءَهُمْ نَذِيرٌ ما زادَهُمْ إِلَّا نُفُوراً "But when a warner came to them, it only increased them in aversion." The "warner" refers to the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم), and aversion (النفور) is distancing and fleeing.

His saying, the Exalted: اسْتِكْباراً فِي الْأَرْضِ وَمَكْرَ السَّيِّئِ وَلا يَحِيقُ الْمَكْرُ السَّيِّئُ إِلَّا بِأَهْلِهِ "due to their acting proudly in the land and their devising of evil schemes; and evil schemes beset only their authors" (35:43). Al-Raghib said: "المكر (al-makr) is turning another away from his intent through a ruse. This is of two types: praiseworthy plotting, which is to seek a beautiful act thereby—and regarding this, He, the Exalted, said: وَاللهُ خَيْرُ الْماكِرِينَ 'and Allah is the best of devisers'—and blameworthy plotting, which is to seek an ugly act thereby. He, the Exalted, said: لا يَحِيقُ الْمَكْرُ السَّيِّئُ إِلَّا بِأَهْلِهِ 'and evil schemes beset only their authors.'" [End of quote].

He also said: "He, the Mighty and Majestic, said: وَلا يَحِيقُ الْمَكْرُ السَّيِّئُ إِلَّا بِأَهْلِهِ 'and evil schemes beset only their authors,' meaning it does not descend upon or afflict [anyone else]. It is said its root is haqqa (حق), then it was transformed like zalla (زل) and zala (زال). It has been recited as فَأَزَلَّهُمَا الشَّيْطَانُ 'Then Satan caused them to slip' and أَزَالَهُمَا 'removed them.' Based on this are dhammahu (ذمه) and dhaamahu (ذامه)." [End of quote].

His saying: اسْتِكْباراً فِي الْأَرْضِ "due to their acting proudly in the land" is an adverb of cause (مفعول لأجله) for His saying نُفُوراً "aversion." That is, they recoiled from him and distanced themselves because of their acting proudly in the land. His saying: وَمَكْرَ السَّيِّئِ "and their devising of evil schemes" is coordinated with اسْتِكْباراً and is likewise an adverb of cause. It is also said it is coordinated with نُفُوراً. The genitive construction here is the attribution of the described to the attribute, as evidenced by His saying a second time: وَلا يَحِيقُ الْمَكْرُ السَّيِّئُ "and evil schemes beset..."

His saying: وَلا يَحِيقُ الْمَكْرُ السَّيِّئُ إِلَّا بِأَهْلِهِ "and evil schemes beset only their authors," meaning that evil plotting does not afflict or descend upon anyone except its author, nor does it settle except in him. For although evil plotting might sometimes cause harm to the one plotted against, it will eventually vanish and not endure. However, its evil effect—insofar as it is an evil plot—remains in the soul of the plotter, will manifest in him, and he will be requited for it, either in this world or certainly in the Hereafter. For this reason, the verse was interpreted in Majma' al-Bayan with the saying: "The meaning is that the requital for the evil plot only descends upon the one who did it."

The statement is sent forth as a proverb, like His saying, the Exalted: إِنَّما بَغْيُكُمْ عَلى أَنْفُسِكُمْ "Your rebellion is only against yourselves" (10:23), and فَمَنْ نَكَثَ فَإِنَّما يَنْكُثُ عَلى نَفْسِهِ "so whoever breaks his oath, breaks it only to his own detriment" (48:10).

His saying: فَهَلْ يَنْظُرُونَ إِلَّا سُنَّتَ الْأَوَّلِينَ "Do they await anything but the precedent of the ancients?" (35:43). Looking (النظر) and awaiting (الانتظار) mean expectation. The fa is for consequence (للتفريع), and the sentence is a conclusion drawn from what preceded it. The interrogation is for denial (للإنكار). The meaning is: since they plotted evil schemes, and evil schemes beset only their authors, they await nothing but the established precedent (السنة) regarding past nations, which is the divine punishment that descended upon them following their plotting and their denial of Allah's signs.

His saying: فَلَنْ تَجِدَ لِسُنَّتِ اللهِ تَبْدِيلاً وَلَنْ تَجِدَ لِسُنَّتِ اللهِ تَحْوِيلاً "and you will never find any change in Allah’s precedent, and you will never find any revision in Allah’s precedent." The "change" (تبديل) of the precedent is to place well-being and blessing in the stead of punishment. Its "revision" (تحويل) is to transfer the punishment from a people who deserve it to others. Allah's precedent accepts neither change nor revision, because He, the Exalted, is upon a straight path (صراط مستقيم); His judgment accepts neither partiality nor exception.

Allah indeed seized these plotting polytheists with punishment on the day of Badr, and the majority of them were killed. The address is to the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) or to every listener.

His saying, the Exalted: أَوَلَمْ يَسِيرُوا فِي الْأَرْضِ فَيَنْظُرُوا كَيْفَ كانَ عاقِبَةُ الَّذِينَ مِنْ قَبْلِهِمْ وَكانُوا أَشَدَّ مِنْهُمْ قُوَّةً "Have they not travelled over the land so that they may observe how was the fate of those who were before them, and they were mightier than them in power?" (35:44). This is a citation of evidence for His precedent prevailing in past nations, who were mightier in power than the polytheists of Mecca, yet Allah seized them with punishment when they plotted and denied.

His saying, the Exalted: وَما كانَ اللهُ لِيُعْجِزَهُ مِنْ شَيْءٍ فِي السَّماواتِ وَلا فِي الْأَرْضِ إِنَّهُ كانَ عَلِيماً قَدِيراً "Nothing in the heavens or on the earth can frustrate Allah. Indeed, He is All-knowing, All-powerful" (35:44). This is a completion of the previous explanation for further warning and intimidation. The gist is: let them be God-conscious (ليتقوا الله) and believe in Him, and let them not plot against Him nor deny Him; for Allah's precedent in that regard is punishment, as witnessed by what occurred to previous nations of destruction and chastisement, even though they were mightier in power than them. Nothing in the heavens or the earth can frustrate Allah, the Glorified, through power or plotting; for He is All-knowing (عليم) in an absolute sense—He is neither heedless nor ignorant such that He could be deceived by a plot or ruse—and He is All-powerful (قدير) in an absolute sense—nothing can resist Him.

His saying, the Exalted: وَلَوْ يُؤاخِذُ اللهُ النَّاسَ بِما كَسَبُوا ما تَرَكَ عَلى ظَهْرِها مِنْ دَابَّةٍ "Were Allah to take mankind to task for what they have earned, He would not leave any living being on its back..." (35:45). What is meant by "taking to task" (المؤاخذة) is the worldly punishment, as indicated by His subsequent saying: وَلكِنْ يُؤَخِّرُهُمْ إِلى أَجَلٍ مُسَمًّى "but He respites them until a specified term..." What is meant by "mankind" (الناس) is all of them, for the verse is preceded by the mention of taking some of them to task—namely, the plotters who deny Allah's signs. What is meant by "what they have earned" (بما كسبوا) are the sins they have committed, by the context of "taking to task," which is punishment. He said in a similar verse in Surah al-Nahl: وَلَوْ يُؤاخِذُ اللهُ النَّاسَ بِظُلْمِهِمْ ما تَرَكَ عَلَيْها مِنْ دَابَّةٍ "Were Allah to take mankind to task for their wrongdoing, He would not leave any living being upon it" (16:61).

What is meant by "its back" (ظهرها) is the back of the earth, because people live upon it, and because the earth was mentioned in the preceding verse.

What is meant by "living being" (دابة) is everything that crawls upon the earth, whether human—male or female, old or young. It is also possible that it means every crawling animal; the destruction of animals other than humans would be because they were created for man, as He, the Exalted, said: خَلَقَ لَكُمْ ما فِي الْأَرْضِ جَمِيعاً "It is He who created for you all that is in the earth" (2:29).

The saying of some that this is due to the ill-omen of sins—and He, the Exalted, said: وَاتَّقُوا فِتْنَةً لا تُصِيبَنَّ الَّذِينَ ظَلَمُوا مِنْكُمْ خَاصَّةً "And beware of a punishment which shall not visit the wrongdoers among you exclusively" (8:25)—is refuted by the fact that the ill-omen of sin does not transcend the sinner to others; He, the Exalted, said: وَلا تَزِرُ وازِرَةٌ وِزْرَ أُخْرى "and no bearer shall bear another’s burden" (35:18). As for the verse وَاتَّقُوا فِتْنَةً لا تُصِيبَنَّ الَّذِينَ ظَلَمُوا مِنْكُمْ خَاصَّةً, its meaning—according to the previous commentary—is the restriction of the trial to those who wronged among them specifically, not its generality to them and others. So refer back to it.

His saying: وَلكِنْ يُؤَخِّرُهُمْ إِلى أَجَلٍ مُسَمًّى "but He respites them until a specified term," which is death or the Resurrection. His saying: فَإِذا جاءَ أَجَلُهُمْ فَإِنَّ اللهَ كانَ بِعِبادِهِ بَصِيراً "so when their term comes, Allah is indeed watchful of His servants," meaning He will requite each according to what they did, for He is watchful of them and knowing of their deeds because they are His servants. How can the Creator be ignorant of His creation, or the Lord of the work of His servant?

It has become clear from the preceding that His saying فَإِنَّ اللهَ كانَ بِعِبادِهِ بَصِيراً "Allah is indeed watchful of His servants" is an instance of placing the cause in the stead of the effect, which is the requital.

The verse—namely His saying, the Exalted: وَلَوْ يُؤاخِذُ اللهُ النَّاسَ "Were Allah to take mankind to task..."—occurs as an answer to a hypothetical question arising from the previous verse. For when He, the Exalted, warned the people of plotting and denial among the polytheists with punishment, cited the precedent of past nations, and mentioned that nothing in the heavens or the earth can frustrate Him, it was as if it were asked: "If nothing in the heavens or the earth can frustrate Him, then why does He leave the rest of mankind as they are in their sins? What prevents Him from taking them to task for what they have earned?" He answered that if He were to take all mankind to task for the sins they earned, just as He takes these plotting deniers to task, He would not leave a single one of them crawling or moving upon the back of the earth. Yet, He, the Glorified, has decreed that they live on the earth and inhabit it, as He said: وَلَكُمْ فِي الْأَرْضِ مُسْتَقَرٌّ وَمَتاعٌ إِلى حِينٍ "and there will be for you on the earth an abode and a provision for a while" (2:36). Thus, He does not take them to task [immediately] but respites them until a specified term, which is death or the Resurrection. When their term arrives, He will deal with them according to what they did; indeed, He is watchful of His servants.

(Narrative Discussion)

In al-Durr al-Manthur, Ibn Abi Hatim narrated via Sufyan from Abu Zakariyya al-Kufi from a man who related to him that the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) said: إياكم والمكر السيئ ـ فإنه لا يحيق المكر السيئ إلا بأهله ـ ولهم من الله طالب — "Beware of evil plotting, for evil schemes beset only their authors, and they have a seeker from Allah [pursuing them]."

In the Tafsir of al-Qummi: My father narrated to me from al-Nawfali from al-Sakuni from Ja'far from his father (عليه السلام) who said: The Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) said: سبق العلم ، وجف القلم ، ومضى القضاء وتم القدر ـ بتحقيق الكتاب ، وتصديق الرسل ، وبالسعادة من الله لمن آمن واتقى ـ وبالشقاء لمن كذب وكفر ، وبالولاية من الله عز وجل للمؤمنين ، وبالبراءة منه المشركين ـ — "Knowledge has preceded, the Pen has dried, the Decree has passed, and the Measure is completed—with the realization of the Book, the confirmation of the messengers, with felicity from Allah for him who believes and is God-conscious, with wretchedness for him who denies and disbelieves, with guardianship (الولاية) from Allah, the Mighty and Majestic, for the believers, and with disavowal from Him for the polytheists."

Then the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) said: إن الله عز وجل يقول : يا ابن آدم بمشيتي كنت أنت ـ الذي تشاء لنفسك ما تشاء ، وبإرادتي كنت أنت الذي تريد لنفسك ما تريد ، وبفضل نعمتي عليك قويت على معصيتي ، وبقوتي وعصمتي وعافيتي أديت إلي فرائضي ـ وأنا أولى بحسناتك منك وأنت أولى بذنبك مني ، الخير مني إليك واصل بما أوليتك به ـ والشر منك إليك بما جنيت جزاء ـ — "Indeed Allah, the Mighty and Majestic, says: 'O son of Adam! By My will, you were the one who wills for yourself what you will; by My volition, you were the one who intends for yourself what you intend; by the grace of My blessing upon you, you were strong enough to disobey Me; and by My strength, protection, and well-being, you fulfilled My obligations to Me. I am more entitled to your good deeds than you are, and you are more entitled to your sin than I am. Good reaches you from Me because of what I have bestowed upon you, and evil reaches you from yourself as a requital for what you have committed.'"

وبكثير من تسلطي لك انطويت على طاعتي ، وبسوء ظنك بي قنطت من رحمتي ـ — "And by much of My empowerment of you, you were disposed toward My obedience; and by your ill-suspicion of Me, you despaired of My mercy."

فلي الحمد والحجة عليك بالبيان ، ولي السبيل عليك بالعصيان ، ولك الجزاء الحسن عندي بالإحسان ، لم أدع تحذيرك ، ولم آخذك عند غرتك ـ وهو قوله عز وجل : «وَلَوْ يُؤاخِذُ اللهُ النَّاسَ بِما كَسَبُواـ ما تَرَكَ عَلى ظَهْرِها مِنْ دَابَّةٍ» ، لم أكلفك فوق طاقتك ، ولم أحملك من الأمانة إلا ما أقررت بها على نفسك ، ورضيت لنفسي منك بما رضيت به لنفسك مني ـ ثم قال عز وجل : «وَلكِنْ يُؤَخِّرُهُمْ إِلى أَجَلٍ مُسَمًّىـ فَإِذا جاءَ أَجَلُهُمْ فَإِنَّ اللهَ كانَ بِعِبادِهِ بَصِيراً» — "To Me belongs the praise and the proof against you through the clarification, and I have a way against you due to the disobedience. For you is the beautiful reward with Me for excellence. I did not omit warning you, nor did I seize you in your state of delusion—and that is His saying, the Mighty and Majestic: 'Were Allah to take mankind to task for what they have earned, He would not leave any living being on its back.' I did not charge you beyond your capacity, nor did I burden you with any trust (الأمانة) except that which you acknowledged for yourself, and I was pleased for Myself from you with what you were pleased with for yourself from Me." Then He, the Mighty and Majestic, said: "but He respites them until a specified term; so when their term comes, Allah is indeed watchful of His servants."
